kids.question: Which tribe of Israel went up first to fight the Canaanites after Joshua died?
kids.explanation: Judges 1:2 — "The LORD said, Judah shall go up: behold, I have delivered the land into his hand."

kids.question: What did the men of Judah do to the king called Adoni-bezek when they caught him?
kids.option_a: They cut off his thumbs and big toes
kids.option_d: They sent him back to his city
kids.explanation: Judges 1:6 — "They cut off his thumbs and his great toes."

kids.question: What did Caleb offer as a reward to whoever captured the city of Debir?
kids.option_b: His daughter Achsah as a wife
kids.option_c: A piece of land by the sea
kids.explanation: Judges 1:12 — "Caleb said, He that smiteth Kirjath-sepher, and taketh it, to him will I give Achsah my daughter to wife."

teens.question: Who captured Kirjath-sepher and received Caleb's daughter Achsah as his wife?
teens.option_a: Othniel the son of Kenaz
teens.option_b: Ehud the son of Gera
teens.option_c: Shamgar the son of Anath
teens.option_d: Caleb's eldest son
teens.explanation: Judges 1:13 — "Othniel the son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother, took it: and he gave him Achsah his daughter to wife."

teens.question: What did Achsah ask her father Caleb to give her, besides the south land he had given?
teens.option_a: Springs of water
teens.option_b: A city of her own
teens.option_c: Flocks of sheep
teens.option_d: An inheritance in Canaan
teens.explanation: Judges 1:15 — "Give me also springs of water. And Caleb gave her the upper springs and the nether springs."

teens.question: What did Adoni-bezek confess had been done to him as a just punishment?
teens.option_a: Seventy kings with thumbs and great toes cut off had gathered scraps under his table
teens.option_b: He had been defeated by his own army
teens.option_c: He had broken the covenant of the gods
teens.option_d: He had wrongly ruled for thirty years
teens.explanation: Judges 1:7 — "Threescore and ten kings, having their thumbs and their great toes cut off, gathered their meat under my table: as I have done, so God hath requited me."

teens.question: The tribe of Benjamin failed to drive out which people from Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: The Jebusites
teens.option_b: The Philistines
teens.option_c: The Amorites
teens.option_d: The Hittites
teens.explanation: Judges 1:21 — "And the children of Benjamin did not drive out the Jebusites that inhabited Jerusalem; but the Jebusites dwell with the children of Benjamin in Jerusalem unto this day."

teens.question: What tribe was put to forced labour because Israel could not drive them out entirely?
teens.option_a: The Canaanites
teens.option_b: The Perizzites
teens.option_c: The Hivites
teens.option_d: The Hittites
teens.explanation: Judges 1:28 — "When Israel was strong, that they put the Canaanites to tribute, and did not utterly drive them out."

adults.question: What divine instruction opened the military campaign described in Judges 1, and who gave it?
adults.option_a: The LORD told Judah to go up first, and confirmed He had delivered the land
adults.option_b: An angel commanded all tribes to march together
adults.option_c: Moses spoke from beyond the grave
adults.option_d: Joshua's written scroll was opened
adults.explanation: Judges 1:2 — "The LORD said, Judah shall go up: behold, I have delivered the land into his hand."

adults.question: Which two tribes joined forces at the opening of Judges 1, and what does this alliance reveal?
adults.option_a: Judah and Simeon, fulfilling their mutual covenant of military brotherhood
adults.option_b: Judah and Levi, honouring priestly leadership
adults.option_c: Judah and Benjamin, defending the southern border
adults.option_d: Judah and Reuben, following the order of encampment
adults.explanation: Judges 1:3 — "Judah said unto Simeon his brother, Come up with me into my lot...So Simeon went with him."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Adoni-bezek's own confession in Judges 1:7?
adults.option_a: He acknowledged the justice of God in repaying him according to his own cruelty
adults.option_b: He converted to the faith of Israel
adults.option_c: He predicted Israel's future apostasy
adults.option_d: He identified Judah as the chosen tribe
adults.explanation: Judges 1:7 — "As I have done, so God hath requited me." His words affirm the principle of lex talionis operating under divine providence.

adults.question: The house of Joseph's capture of Bethel is introduced in Judges 1:22. What intelligence method helped them take the city?
adults.option_a: A man from the city showed them the entrance in exchange for his family's safety
adults.option_b: They used a secret tunnel under the walls
adults.option_c: A spy had been living there for a year
adults.option_d: They bribed the city's gatekeeper
adults.explanation: Judges 1:24-25 — "The spies saw a man come forth out of the city, and they said...Shew us...the entrance...and we will shew thee mercy."

adults.question: According to Judges 1:19, what military advantage did the inhabitants of the valley have that hindered Judah?
adults.option_a: Chariots of iron
adults.option_b: Superior archery from high walls
adults.option_c: Alliances with surrounding nations
adults.option_d: Fortified underground cities
adults.explanation: Judges 1:19 — "He could not drive out the inhabitants of the valley, because they had chariots of iron."

adults.question: List three tribes mentioned in Judges 1 that failed to drive out Canaanites and instead allowed them to dwell among them.
adults.option_a: Manasseh, Ephraim, and Zebulun
adults.option_b: Judah, Issachar, and Naphtali
adults.option_c: Reuben, Gad, and Asher
adults.option_d: Dan, Levi, and Benjamin
adults.explanation: Judges 1:27-33 — Manasseh (v.27), Ephraim (v.29), Zebulun (v.30), Asher (v.31), and Naphtali (v.33) all failed to expel Canaanites.

adults.question: What happened to the Amorites when they pressed Dan back into the hill country?
adults.option_a: The Amorites forced Dan into the hill country and would not allow them to come down to the valley
adults.option_b: Dan allied with Judah and drove them back
adults.option_c: The Amorites were eventually put to tribute by the house of Joseph
adults.option_d: Dan appealed to the LORD and received a victory
adults.explanation: Judges 1:34-35 — "The Amorites forced the children of Dan into the mountain: for they would not suffer them to come down to the valley." The Amorites were later put to tribute by the house of Joseph.

adults.question: What is the literary purpose of the repeated phrase "did not drive out" throughout Judges 1, and how does it set up the rest of the book?
adults.option_a: It establishes the pattern of incomplete obedience that will lead to the cycles of apostasy and oppression throughout Judges
adults.option_b: It records a census of Canaanite survivors
adults.option_c: It explains the geography of Canaan
adults.option_d: It justifies Israel's tolerance of pagan neighbours
adults.explanation: Judges 1:19,21,27,28,29,30,31,33 — The repeated phrase forms a theological indictment: Israel's failure to obey the ban (herem) directly causes the crises of Judges 2 onward.

kids.question: Where did the angel of the LORD appear to all the children of Israel in Judges 2?
kids.explanation: Judges 2:1 — "And an angel of the LORD came up from Gilgal to Bochim."

kids.question: What did the people do when they heard the angel's message at Bochim?
kids.option_a: They lifted up their voice and wept
kids.option_b: They sang a song of praise
kids.explanation: Judges 2:4 — "The people lifted up their voice, and wept."

kids.question: What did the LORD give Israel when they were in trouble from their enemies?
kids.explanation: Judges 2:16 — "Nevertheless the LORD raised up judges, which delivered them out of the hand of those that spoiled them."

teens.question: What covenant violation did the angel of the LORD rebuke Israel for at Bochim?
teens.option_a: Making a league with the inhabitants of the land and not throwing down their altars
teens.option_b: Failing to circumcise their children
teens.option_c: Refusing to offer sacrifices
teens.option_d: Worshipping at Shiloh instead of Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Judges 2:2 — "Ye shall make no league with the inhabitants of this land; ye shall throw down their altars: but ye have not obeyed my voice."

teens.question: How old was Joshua when he died, according to Judges 2?
teens.option_a: One hundred and ten years old
teens.option_b: One hundred and twenty years old
teens.option_c: Ninety years old
teens.option_d: One hundred years old
teens.explanation: Judges 2:8 — "Joshua the son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died, being an hundred and ten years old."

teens.question: What generation after Joshua did not know the LORD or the works He had done for Israel?
teens.option_a: The generation that arose after them
teens.option_b: The generation of Joshua's children
teens.option_c: The third generation after Moses
teens.option_d: The Levites who replaced Phinehas
teens.explanation: Judges 2:10 — "There arose another generation after them, which knew not the LORD, nor yet the works which he had done for Israel."

teens.question: According to Judges 2, what did Israel do that provoked the LORD to anger?
teens.option_a: They forsook the LORD and served Baal and Ashtaroth
teens.option_b: They refused to pay tithes to the priests
teens.option_c: They intermarried with the Philistines
teens.option_d: They stopped going to war against the Canaanites
teens.explanation: Judges 2:13 — "They forsook the LORD, and served Baal and Ashtaroth."

teens.question: In the cycle described in Judges 2, what happened when a judge died?
teens.option_a: Israel returned to idolatry and behaved worse than their fathers
teens.option_b: A new judge immediately arose
teens.option_c: Israel kept the covenant for a generation
teens.option_d: The LORD sent a prophet to warn them
teens.explanation: Judges 2:19 — "When the judge was dead...they returned, and corrupted themselves more than their fathers."

adults.question: What is the theological name scholars give to the pattern introduced in Judges 2:11-19, and what are its stages?
adults.option_a: The Deuteronomic Cycle: sin, oppression, cry, deliverance by a judge, rest, then relapse
adults.option_b: The Covenant Spiral: blessing, testing, failure, punishment, and renewal
adults.option_c: The Priestly Pattern: sacrifice, atonement, cleansing, and restoration
adults.option_d: The Prophetic Sequence: warning, rejection, exile, and return
adults.explanation: Judges 2:11-19 outlines the recurring cycle: Israel sins (v.11-13), the LORD delivers them to enemies (v.14), they cry out (v.18), the LORD raises a judge (v.16), rest comes, and then they relapse (v.19).

adults.question: What specific reason does Judges 2:22 give for the LORD leaving nations in the land after Joshua?
adults.option_a: To prove Israel — whether they would keep the way of the LORD or not
adults.option_b: To punish Israel for their sins for forty years
adults.option_c: To provide labourers for Israel's building projects
adults.option_d: To fulfil the covenant with the Canaanites
adults.explanation: Judges 2:22 — "That through them I may prove Israel, whether they will keep the way of the LORD to walk therein, as their fathers did keep it, or not."

adults.question: What does the angel's statement "I will not drive them out from before you" in Judges 2:3 reveal about God's covenantal response?
adults.option_a: God conditionally withdrew His promise of conquest due to Israel's breach of covenant terms
adults.option_b: God permanently abandoned Israel as His people
adults.option_c: God transferred the promise to another nation
adults.option_d: God was testing the angel's authority
adults.explanation: Judges 2:3 — "I also said, I will not drive them out...but they shall be as thorns in your sides, and their gods shall be a snare." This is a covenant curse responding to covenant breach.

adults.question: Judges 2:17 says Israel would not hearken unto their judges but went a whoring after other gods. What does this marital metaphor for idolatry convey?
adults.option_a: Israel's covenant with God was like a marriage, and idolatry was adultery against that covenant
adults.option_b: The judges themselves were immoral men
adults.option_c: Canaanite fertility rites involved literal immorality
adults.option_d: The metaphor only describes the women of Israel
adults.explanation: Judges 2:17 — "Yet they would not hearken unto their judges, but they went a whoring after other gods." The marriage covenant metaphor (used throughout the prophets) frames idolatry as covenant unfaithfulness.

adults.question: What does Judges 2:18 reveal about the emotional response of the LORD toward His people's suffering under oppressors?
adults.option_a: It repented the LORD because of their groanings — He was moved with compassion
adults.option_b: The LORD remained unmoved until Israel repented fully
adults.option_c: The LORD delegated His grief to the angels
adults.option_d: The LORD punished the oppressors without regarding Israel's suffering
adults.explanation: Judges 2:18 — "It repented the LORD because of their groanings by reason of them that oppressed them and vexed them."

adults.question: Where was Joshua buried, and what is the significance of that location?
adults.option_a: In the border of his inheritance in Timnath-heres in mount Ephraim, tying his burial to the land he helped conquer
adults.option_b: In Shiloh beside the tabernacle, near the ark
adults.option_c: In Hebron alongside Caleb, his faithful companion
adults.option_d: In the valley of Jordan where Israel crossed
adults.explanation: Judges 2:9 — "They buried him in the border of his inheritance in Timnath-heres, in the mount of Ephraim." His burial in the promised land sealed his identity as its conqueror.

adults.question: According to Judges 2:14, what two specific consequences did God's anger bring upon Israel?
adults.option_a: He delivered them into the hands of spoilers and sold them into the hands of their enemies
adults.option_b: He sent famine and plague upon the land
adults.option_c: He withdrew the ark and removed the priesthood
adults.option_d: He caused division between the tribes and civil war
adults.explanation: Judges 2:14 — "The anger of the LORD was hot against Israel, and he delivered them into the hands of spoilers that spoiled them, and he sold them into the hands of their enemies round about."

adults.question: What nations does Judges 2:23 say the LORD left to remain in Canaan, and what dual purpose did this serve?
adults.option_a: He left them to test Israel and because He would not drive them out speedily, leaving them in the hand of Joshua — a bridge to chapter 3's list
adults.option_b: He left only the Philistines because no judge could defeat them
adults.option_c: He left the Jebusites and Canaanites as permanent residents
adults.option_d: He left the Amorites to teach Israel chariot warfare
adults.explanation: Judges 2:23 — "Therefore the LORD left those nations, without driving them out hastily; neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua." This bridges to the testing purpose elaborated in Judges 3:1-4.

kids.question: Who was the first judge God raised up to save Israel?
kids.explanation: Judges 3:9 — "The LORD raised up a deliverer to the children of Israel, who delivered them, even Othniel the son of Kenaz."

kids.question: What special characteristic did Ehud the judge have?
kids.explanation: Judges 3:15 — "The LORD raised them up a deliverer, Ehud the son of Gera, a Benjamite, a man lefthanded."

kids.question: What did Shamgar use to kill six hundred Philistines?
kids.explanation: Judges 3:31 — "Shamgar the son of Anath...slew of the Philistines six hundred men with an ox goad."

teens.question: Who was Othniel, and how was he related to Caleb?
teens.option_a: He was Caleb's younger brother's son — his nephew
teens.option_b: He was Caleb's eldest son
teens.option_c: He was Caleb's son-in-law only, with no blood relation
teens.option_d: He was from the tribe of Judah but unrelated to Caleb
teens.explanation: Judges 3:9 — "Othniel the son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother." He was Caleb's nephew and also Caleb's son-in-law (Judges 1:13).

teens.question: Which king oppressed Israel for eight years before Othniel delivered them?
teens.option_a: Chushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia
teens.option_b: Eglon king of Moab
teens.option_c: Jabin king of Canaan
teens.option_d: Cushan king of Edom
teens.explanation: Judges 3:8 — "The LORD sold them into the hand of Chushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia: and the children of Israel served Chushan-rishathaim eight years."

teens.question: How long did Ehud serve as judge, indicated by the land having rest?
teens.option_a: Eighty years
teens.option_b: Forty years
teens.option_c: Twenty years
teens.option_d: Sixty years
teens.explanation: Judges 3:30 — "So Moab was subdued that day under the hand of Israel. And the land had rest fourscore years." Eighty years of peace followed Ehud's deliverance.

teens.question: What did Ehud say to King Eglon to get a private audience with him?
teens.option_a: I have a secret errand unto thee, O king
teens.option_b: I bring tribute from Israel to honour thee
teens.option_c: I have a message from God for thee alone
teens.option_d: I must speak to thee about peace between our peoples
teens.explanation: Judges 3:19 — "Ehud said, I have a secret errand unto thee, O king: who said, Keep silence. And all that stood by him went out from him."

teens.question: What detail about Ehud's dagger is given, and how did it aid his escape?
teens.option_a: It was eighteen inches long and hidden on his right thigh; Eglon's fat closed over it so Ehud did not pull it out
teens.option_b: It was a double-edged sword hidden under his robe
teens.option_c: It was a bronze knife concealed in his sandal
teens.option_d: It was strapped to his back beneath his garment
teens.explanation: Judges 3:16,22 — The dagger was a cubit long, strapped on his right thigh. "The haft also went in after the blade; and the fat closed upon the blade...he could not draw the dagger out."

adults.question: What is the purpose stated in Judges 3:2 for leaving nations in Canaan to test Israel?
adults.option_a: That the generations of Israel that had not known the wars of Canaan might be taught war
adults.option_b: That Israel would remain humble before the LORD
adults.option_c: That the land would not be desolate without inhabitants
adults.option_d: That Israel's population would not grow too quickly
adults.explanation: Judges 3:2 — "Only that the generations of the children of Israel might know, to teach them war, at the least such as before knew nothing thereof."

adults.question: What five nations are specifically listed in Judges 3:3 as the ones left to test Israel?
adults.option_a: Five lords of the Philistines, Canaanites, Sidonians, Hivites of Lebanon, and inhabitants of mount Baal-hermon
adults.option_b: Philistines, Moabites, Ammonites, Amorites, and Jebusites
adults.option_c: Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, Canaanites, and Girgashites
adults.option_d: Midianites, Amalekites, Edomites, Moabites, and Egyptians
adults.explanation: Judges 3:3 — "Five lords of the Philistines, and all the Canaanites, and the Sidonians, and the Hivites that dwelt in mount Lebanon, from mount Baal-hermon unto the entering in of Hamath."

adults.question: What is the significance of Eglon's obesity in the Ehud narrative?
adults.option_a: The fat closing over the dagger explains why the weapon was not retrieved and prevented discovery; it also satirises Moabite excess
adults.option_b: It fulfilled a prophecy about fat kings being overthrown
adults.option_c: It symbolised the wealth of Moab that Israel desired
adults.option_d: It showed that Eglon was a man of great feasting who lacked military alertness
adults.explanation: Judges 3:22 — "The fat closed upon the blade...he could not draw the dagger out." The detail is both practical (hiding the crime) and satirical (Eglon's bloated oppression literally consuming itself).

adults.question: How did Ehud escape after killing Eglon, and how did Eglon's servants delay the discovery of his body?
adults.option_a: Ehud locked the parlour doors from outside; servants assumed Eglon was covering his feet and waited until they were ashamed
adults.option_b: Ehud disguised himself as a servant and walked out; servants found the body after midnight
adults.option_c: Ehud escaped through a window and the servants were forbidden to enter the chamber
adults.option_d: Ehud hid in the garden and left after dark
adults.explanation: Judges 3:23-25 — "Ehud went forth through the porch...and locked the doors...His servants...said, Surely he covereth his feet in his summer chamber...they were ashamed: and behold their lord was fallen down dead."

adults.question: What rallying device did Ehud use to gather Israel's troops, and what was the geographic strategy for the battle?
adults.option_a: He blew a trumpet in mount Ephraim and led Israel to seize the fords of Jordan, cutting off Moab's retreat
adults.option_b: He sent messengers to all tribes and attacked at night from the hills
adults.option_c: He lit signal fires on the heights of Benjamin
adults.option_d: He gathered the elders and marched straight to the Jordan plain
adults.explanation: Judges 3:27-28 — "He blew a trumpet in the mountain of Ephraim...Follow after me: for the LORD hath delivered your enemies the Moabites into your hand. And they went down after him, and took the fords of Jordan toward Moab."

adults.question: How many Moabites were killed in the battle following Ehud's assassination of Eglon?
adults.option_a: About ten thousand men — all lusty and strong, no man escaped
adults.option_b: Five thousand soldiers at the Jordan fords
adults.option_c: Twenty thousand over three days of battle
adults.option_d: An uncounted multitude, described as the whole army of Moab
adults.explanation: Judges 3:29 — "They slew of Moab at that time about ten thousand men, all lusty, and all men of valour; and there escaped not a man."

adults.question: What does the name Chushan-rishathaim mean, and why might the author have included its meaning?
adults.option_a: Cushan of double wickedness — a theological epithet emphasising his evil character and divine justice in his defeat
adults.option_b: Cushan the mighty king — celebrating his temporary power
adults.option_c: Cushan of the two rivers — describing his Mesopotamian homeland
adults.option_d: Cushan the oppressor — simply a title of his role over Israel
adults.explanation: The name Chushan-rishathaim is widely understood as meaning "Cushan of double wickedness," serving as an ironic characterisation that frames his oppression as deserving the divine response described in Judges 3:10.

adults.question: How does Othniel's judgeship in Judges 3:7-11 function as the paradigmatic or model example of the Deuteronomic cycle?
adults.option_a: It presents all four stages in their clearest form — sin, servitude, supplication, salvation — without complicating details, establishing the template for all subsequent judge narratives
adults.option_b: It is the longest judge narrative and therefore sets the standard by length
adults.option_c: It is unique because the Spirit of God is mentioned, making it more important than the others
adults.option_d: It is the only narrative where the judge is from the tribe of Judah, setting the royal template
adults.explanation: Judges 3:7-11 is the shortest and most formulaic judge narrative: sin (v.7), anger and servitude (v.8), cry and raising of deliverer (v.9), Spirit empowerment (v.10), conquest and rest (v.11) — textbook Deuteronomic cycle.

kids.question: Who was the prophetess that judged Israel in Judges 4?
kids.explanation: Judges 4:4 — "Deborah, a prophetess, the wife of Lapidoth, she judged Israel at that time."

kids.question: Who was the commander of the enemy army that Deborah and Barak fought against?
kids.explanation: Judges 4:2 — "The captain of whose host was Sisera, which dwelt in Harosheth of the Gentiles."

kids.question: Who killed Sisera by driving a tent peg through his head?
kids.option_d: A woman from the city walls
kids.explanation: Judges 4:21 — "Jael...took a nail of the tent, and took an hammer in her hand, and went softly unto him, and smote the nail into his temples."

teens.question: Under which king did Israel suffer oppression when Deborah judged, and how many years did it last?
teens.option_a: Jabin king of Canaan, for twenty years
teens.option_b: Eglon king of Moab, for eighteen years
teens.option_c: Chushan-rishathaim, for eight years
teens.option_d: The Midianites, for seven years
teens.explanation: Judges 4:2-3 — "The LORD sold them into the hand of Jabin king of Canaan...And the children of Israel cried unto the LORD: for he had nine hundred chariots of iron; and twenty years he mightily oppressed the children of Israel."

teens.question: What condition did Barak place on leading the army against Sisera?
teens.option_a: If Deborah would go with him, he would go; but if not, he would not go
teens.option_b: If God would speak from heaven he would obey
teens.option_c: If ten thousand men volunteered he would lead them
teens.option_d: If Deborah would fast for seven days he would trust the victory
teens.explanation: Judges 4:8 — "Barak said unto her, If thou wilt go with me, then I will go: but if thou wilt not go with me, then I will not go."

teens.question: What consequence did Deborah prophesy for Barak's hesitation?
teens.option_a: The honour of the victory would go to a woman, not to him
teens.option_b: He would lose his position as commander
teens.option_c: He would be captured in battle
teens.option_d: He would be struck with fear at the critical moment
teens.explanation: Judges 4:9 — "Deborah said...the journey that thou takest shall not be for thine honour; for the LORD shall sell Sisera into the hand of a woman."

teens.question: How did the LORD rout Sisera's army according to Judges 4:15?
teens.option_a: The LORD discomfited Sisera and all his chariots and all his host with the edge of the sword
teens.option_b: The LORD sent rain to flood the plain
teens.option_c: The LORD sent fire from heaven upon the chariots
teens.option_d: The LORD caused Sisera's soldiers to fight one another
teens.explanation: Judges 4:15 — "The LORD discomfited Sisera, and all his chariots, and all his host, with the edge of the sword before Barak."

teens.question: How did Jael lure Sisera into her tent before killing him?
teens.option_a: She invited him in, gave him milk to drink, covered him, and told him not to fear
teens.option_b: She pretended to be wounded and asked for his help
teens.option_c: She offered him food and told him she had seen his enemies pass
teens.option_d: She appeared to be a servant at the city gate and guided him to a hiding place
teens.explanation: Judges 4:18-19 — "Jael went out to meet Sisera, and said...Turn in, my lord...And she covered him...And he said...Give me, I pray thee, a little water to drink...And she opened a bottle of milk, and gave him drink, and covered him."

adults.question: What does the detail that Deborah judged Israel "under the palm tree of Deborah between Ramah and Beth-el" reveal about her judicial function?
adults.option_a: She held a fixed, public location where Israelites came to her for judgment, indicating a formal judicial role distinct from the military deliverers
adults.option_b: She was a nature worshipper who held court outdoors
adults.option_c: She was too poor to have a proper building
adults.option_d: The palm tree was a sacred site associated with the tabernacle
adults.explanation: Judges 4:5 — "She dwelt under the palm tree of Deborah between Ramah and Beth-el in mount Ephraim: and the children of Israel came up to her for judgment." This establishes her as a civil judge, not merely a military leader.

adults.question: What is the theological irony in Sisera fleeing on foot to Jael's tent, given the earlier emphasis on his nine hundred iron chariots?
adults.option_a: The very military might Israel feared most (iron chariots) proved useless, and their commander fled on foot, showing that the LORD's power trumps all human military advantage
adults.option_b: It shows that Sisera had planned the escape route in advance
adults.option_c: It demonstrates that iron chariots could not cross certain terrain
adults.option_d: It fulfilled a specific prophecy about enemy generals fleeing
adults.explanation: Judges 4:3 established nine hundred iron chariots as the source of Israel's oppression; Judges 4:15,17 show Sisera abandoning them and fleeing on foot, inverting Israel's greatest fear.

adults.question: What covenant context explains Jael's act of killing Sisera, despite her husband Heber's peace with Jabin?
adults.option_a: Heber had separated from the Kenites and made his own peace; Jael acted on loyalty to Israel — whose God she apparently honoured — overriding her husband's political alliance
adults.option_b: Jael was under a vow to destroy all enemies of God's people regardless of her husband's decisions
adults.option_c: Jael killed Sisera because he had personally wronged her family
adults.option_d: The text does not provide sufficient context to evaluate her motive
adults.explanation: Judges 4:11,17 — Heber the Kenite had separated from Moses' father-in-law's clan and made peace with Jabin. Jael's act defied that alliance, and Judges 5:24 celebrates her as "blessed above women," indicating the narrator views her as acting in fidelity to Israel's God.

adults.question: What does the phrase "the LORD discomfited Sisera" (Judges 4:15) indicate about who receives credit for the victory?
adults.option_a: Divine agency is the ultimate cause of the military rout, with human actors (Barak, Jael) as instruments, fulfilling Deborah's prophetic assurance in Judges 4:7
adults.option_b: Barak's superior strategy was vindicated by God's blessing
adults.option_c: The phrase is a formulaic ending with no theological weight
adults.option_d: It means God caused a storm but the soldiers did all the fighting
adults.explanation: Judges 4:7 Deborah declared "I will deliver him into thine hand"; Judges 4:15 confirms "the LORD discomfited Sisera." The divine passive frames the whole narrative as God's victory, not Israel's alone.

adults.question: How does Barak's inclusion in the Hebrews 11 faith hall of fame (Hebrews 11:32) reframe the judgment implied by Deborah's prophecy in Judges 4:9?
adults.option_a: His faith was genuine even if imperfect; his hesitation did not disqualify him but the honour of the killing blow went to Jael as prophesied
adults.option_b: Barak replaced Deborah as the primary hero in later tradition
adults.option_c: The New Testament passage contradicts the Old Testament account
adults.option_d: Barak's inclusion suggests the prophecy was not fulfilled
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:32 lists Barak among faith heroes. This suggests that his condition in Judges 4:8 was an expression of faith needing Deborah's prophetic presence, not cowardice — yet God's word through Deborah (Judges 4:9) still stood.

adults.question: What geographic and tactical significance did the river Kishon hold in the battle?
adults.option_a: According to Judges 5:21 it swept away Sisera's troops, likely flooding and immobilising the iron chariots that were his chief military asset
adults.option_b: It served as Israel's defensive barrier protecting Barak's camp
adults.option_c: It was a sacred river associated with divine appearances
adults.option_d: It divided the Canaanite forces into two groups
adults.explanation: Judges 4:7 directs the battle to "the river Kishon"; Judges 5:21 — "The river of Kishon swept them away." The flooding Kishon likely neutralised Sisera's iron chariots, transforming his advantage into a liability.

adults.question: The clan of Heber the Kenite descended from Moses' father-in-law. What does the mention of Heber's separation in Judges 4:11 foreshadow within the chapter?
adults.option_a: It explains why Sisera would consider Jael's tent a safe neutral refuge — setting up the dramatic irony of his death there
adults.option_b: It introduces a sub-plot about Kenite politics
adults.option_c: It explains why Jael had the skills to use a tent peg as a weapon
adults.option_d: It provides a genealogical link between Deborah and Jael
adults.explanation: Judges 4:11 establishes Heber's separate peace with Jabin, which in Judges 4:17 Sisera relies upon for safety. The narrator's advance notice creates dramatic irony: the supposed safe house becomes his tomb.

adults.question: How does chapter 4 illustrate the theme of reversal of expectations that runs through the book of Judges?
adults.option_a: A woman judges Israel, a woman is given the military honour, and the feared iron chariot commander dies ignominiously by a tent peg in a woman's hand
adults.option_b: A Canaanite king becomes a worshipper of the LORD
adults.option_c: The smallest tribe leads the greatest military victory
adults.option_d: An old man defeats a young warrior in single combat
adults.explanation: Judges 4 systematically inverts military and social expectations: Deborah (female) judges, Barak (military leader) defers, Sisera (iron chariot general) flees on foot, and Jael (homemaker) executes the final blow with domestic tools (Judges 4:4, 8, 17, 21).

kids.question: Who sang a song of praise after Israel defeated Sisera?
kids.explanation: Judges 5:1 — "Then sang Deborah and Barak the son of Abinoam on that day."

kids.question: What does the Song of Deborah say the stars did in the battle?
kids.option_a: They fought from their courses against Sisera
kids.option_b: They fell from heaven upon the enemy
kids.option_c: They guided Israel through the night
kids.option_d: They shone so brightly the enemy was blinded
kids.explanation: Judges 5:20 — "They fought from heaven; the stars in their courses fought against Sisera."

kids.question: What city was cursed by the angel of the LORD in the Song of Deborah?
kids.explanation: Judges 5:23 — "Curse ye Meroz, said the angel of the LORD, curse ye bitterly the inhabitants thereof; because they came not to the help of the LORD."

teens.question: How does the Song of Deborah describe the state of the roads and villages before Israel's deliverance?
teens.option_a: The highways were unoccupied and the villages ceased until Deborah arose
teens.option_b: The roads were filled with enemies and the villages burned
teens.option_c: The paths were blocked by iron chariots for forty years
teens.option_d: The villages paid tribute and the roads were patrolled by Canaanites
teens.explanation: Judges 5:6-7 — "In the days of Shamgar...the highways were unoccupied, and the travellers walked through byways...The villages ceased in Israel, until that I Deborah arose."

teens.question: Which tribes are praised in the Song of Deborah for willingly offering themselves to battle?
teens.option_a: Ephraim, Benjamin, Machir, Zebulun, Issachar, and Naphtali
teens.option_b: Judah, Simeon, Dan, and Reuben
teens.option_c: Gad, Asher, and Levi
teens.option_d: Manasseh, Joseph, and Benjamin
teens.explanation: Judges 5:14-15,18 — Ephraim, Benjamin, Machir, Zebulun, Issachar, and Naphtali are praised for joining the battle.

teens.question: Which tribes are rebuked in the Song of Deborah for not coming to help?
teens.option_a: Reuben, Gilead, Dan, and Asher
teens.option_b: Judah, Levi, and Simeon
teens.option_c: Gad, Manasseh, and Ephraim
teens.option_d: Benjamin and Issachar
teens.explanation: Judges 5:15-17 — "In the divisions of Reuben there were great searchings of heart...Gilead abode beyond Jordan: and why did Dan remain in ships? Asher continued on the sea shore."

teens.question: How does the song describe the death blow Jael gave to Sisera?
teens.option_a: She smote off his head, she wounded and struck him down between her feet he bowed and fell
teens.option_b: She drove the peg through his neck as he slept on his side
teens.option_c: She struck him with the hammer as he stood drinking
teens.option_d: She stabbed him with his own sword as he lay sleeping
teens.explanation: Judges 5:26-27 — "She put her hand to the nail...and smote Sisera, she smote off his head...At her feet he bowed, he fell: where he bowed, there he fell down dead."

teens.question: What was Sisera's mother doing as the song ends, and what is the poignant irony?
teens.option_a: She was watching through the window for his return, expecting plunder — unaware he was dead
teens.option_b: She was celebrating victory with the other women of Canaan
teens.option_c: She was already mourning, having dreamed of his death
teens.option_d: She was sending a messenger to ask about the delay in battle
teens.explanation: Judges 5:28-30 — "The mother of Sisera looked out at a window...Why is his chariot so long in coming?...Have they not sped? have they not divided the prey?" The irony is that she anticipates his triumph while he lies dead.

adults.question: What is the literary genre and approximate date of the Song of Deborah, and why is it significant for biblical scholarship?
adults.option_a: It is archaic Hebrew victory poetry, widely dated to the 12th century BC, making it among the oldest pieces of biblical literature and a primary historical source for the period of the judges
adults.option_b: It is a late editorial composition inserted to interpret the prose narrative
adults.option_c: It is a liturgical hymn composed for temple worship during the monarchy
adults.option_d: It is a translation from Canaanite victory hymns adapted for Israelite use
adults.explanation: The Song of Deborah (Judges 5) is widely regarded by scholars as archaic Hebrew poetry of high antiquity, its linguistic features (archaic pronoun forms, unusual vocabulary) suggesting composition close to the events of the 12th century BC.

adults.question: The Song of Deborah in Judges 5:4-5 describes the LORD coming from Seir and Edom with cosmic effects. What theological tradition does this invoke?
adults.option_a: The theophanic warrior tradition — the LORD marches from his southern holy mountain bringing storm, earthquake, and victory for His people
adults.option_b: A reference to Edom's conversion to Israelite religion
adults.option_c: A geographical explanation of how Deborah's forces moved
adults.option_d: A polemic against Edomite deities
adults.explanation: Judges 5:4-5 — "LORD, when thou wentest out of Seir...the earth trembled...the mountains melted." This is the theophanic warrior pattern seen also in Deut. 33:2, Hab. 3:3, and Psalm 68 — God marching from Sinai/Edom to fight for Israel.

adults.question: What does the curse on Meroz (Judges 5:23) reveal about the theology of holy war in Israel?
adults.option_a: Failure to join the LORD's battle when called is itself a covenant violation worthy of curse, not merely civic cowardice
adults.option_b: Meroz was a Canaanite city allied with Sisera
adults.option_c: The curse was later lifted when Meroz joined subsequent battles
adults.option_d: Only the tribe of Levi was exempt from military service in holy war
adults.explanation: Judges 5:23 — "Curse ye Meroz...because they came not to the help of the LORD, to the help of the LORD against the mighty." The curse implies that participation in holy war was a covenant obligation; non-participation was apostasy.

adults.question: How does Judges 5:31 function as a doxological conclusion to the Song, and what petition does it contain?
adults.option_a: It prays that all God's enemies perish as Sisera did, while those who love Him shine as the sun in its strength — framing the victory as a paradigm for eschatological hope
adults.option_b: It summarises the casualties of the battle and calls for mourning
adults.option_c: It warns Israel not to repeat their sins lest they be oppressed again
adults.option_d: It records the number of years of peace that followed
adults.explanation: Judges 5:31 — "So let all thine enemies perish, O LORD: but let them that love him be as the sun when he goeth forth in his might." This doxological close transforms a historical victory into an eschatological prayer.

adults.question: What does the Song's vivid portrayal of Sisera's mother's futile waiting (Judges 5:28-30) accomplish literarily and theologically?
adults.option_a: It humanises the enemy and shows the tragedy of war while simultaneously celebrating God's justice — the very plunder she expects never comes because her son was the one judged
adults.option_b: It creates sympathy for Canaanite women as victims of Israelite aggression
adults.option_c: It is an editorial addition to balance the Israelite celebration with Canaanite grief
adults.option_d: It fulfils Deborah's prophecy that the honour would go to a woman
adults.explanation: Judges 5:28-30 gives Sisera's mother a voice anticipating her son's return with plunder. The reader knows Sisera is dead. The passage creates tragic irony and implicitly addresses the plunder theme that runs through Judges, showing the oppressor's plan (v.30, "a damsel or two; to Sisera a prey") reversed.

adults.question: What does the phrase "the stars in their courses fought against Sisera" (Judges 5:20) indicate about the song's cosmological theology?
adults.option_a: The entire created order — heavenly bodies and the Kishon river — participated in Israel's deliverance, presenting the battle as a cosmic event orchestrated by the LORD of hosts
adults.option_b: The battle literally involved a meteor shower
adults.option_c: It is a metaphor for Israel's tribal confederacy rallying from across the region
adults.option_d: It reflects Canaanite astral theology that the poet ironically co-opts
adults.explanation: Judges 5:20-21 — "The stars in their courses fought against Sisera. The river of Kishon swept them away." Both heavenly (stars) and earthly (river) elements are mobilised, presenting the LORD as commander of the whole cosmos (a "hosts" theology), not merely a tribal deity.

adults.question: In the Song, Deborah calls herself "a mother in Israel" (Judges 5:7). What does this title claim about her role?
adults.option_a: She functioned as a nurturing, protective authority figure for the whole nation — a leadership role that transcended gender conventions of the ancient Near East
adults.option_b: She was literally the oldest woman in Israel at the time
adults.option_c: She was the mother of Barak the commander
adults.option_d: She adopted orphans left by the Canaanite wars
adults.explanation: Judges 5:7 — "Until that I Deborah arose, that I arose a mother in Israel." "Mother in Israel" is a title of respected national authority (cf. 2 Sam. 20:19 for a city), here applied to Deborah as Israel's protector and deliverer.

adults.question: How does the Song of Deborah's treatment of the tribes who did not come (Reuben, Dan, Asher, Gilead) reflect the political reality of the tribal confederacy in the period of the judges?
adults.option_a: The song reveals a loose, voluntary confederacy where tribes pursued their own economic interests — Reuben with his flocks, Dan with his ships, Asher with his trade — rather than a unified national army under central command
adults.option_b: It shows that only southern tribes were ever loyal to the covenant
adults.option_c: It reflects punishment for these tribes' earlier apostasy during Othniel's time
adults.option_d: It indicates these tribes were geographically too distant to participate
adults.explanation: Judges 5:15-17 — Reuben's "great searchings of heart," Dan remaining "in ships," Asher on "the sea shore." The tribal interests portrayed (pastoralism, maritime trade) show Israel as a confederacy of economically diverse groups whose unity was voluntary and fragile.

kids.question: Who appeared to Gideon under the oak tree in Ophrah?
kids.explanation: Judges 6:11-12 — "There came an angel of the LORD, and sat under an oak which was in Ophrah...And the LORD looked upon him, and said, The LORD is with thee, thou mighty man of valour."

kids.question: What were the Midianites doing to Israel that made them very poor?
kids.option_a: Destroying their crops and taking their animals every harvest
kids.option_d: Blocking their roads and stopping trade
kids.explanation: Judges 6:4-5 — "They...destroyed the increase of the earth...and left no sustenance for Israel, neither sheep, nor ox, nor ass."

kids.question: What test did Gideon use to confirm that God would save Israel through him?
kids.option_a: The fleece test — dew on fleece and dry ground, then dry fleece and wet ground
kids.option_b: He threw a stone and asked God to make it float
kids.option_c: He prayed for fire from heaven on an altar
kids.option_d: He asked the sun to stand still for one hour
kids.explanation: Judges 6:36-40 — Gideon asked God to put dew only on a fleece (v.37-38), then to make only the ground wet and the fleece dry (v.39-40). Both signs were given.

teens.question: How long did the Midianite oppression last, and what caused God to send a prophet to rebuke Israel?
teens.option_a: Seven years; Israel cried to the LORD because of Midian's destruction
teens.option_b: Twenty years; Israel stopped offering sacrifices
teens.option_c: Forty years; Israel worshipped Baal openly
teens.option_d: Twelve years; Israel paid tribute to Midian
teens.explanation: Judges 6:1-2 — "The children of Israel did evil in the sight of the LORD: and the LORD delivered them into the hand of Midian seven years." Judges 6:6-8 — they cried to the LORD and He sent a prophet.

teens.question: Where was Gideon threshing wheat when the angel of the LORD appeared to him, and why was this location significant?
teens.option_a: In the winepress, to hide it from the Midianites
teens.option_b: On the threshing floor on a hilltop to catch the wind
teens.option_c: In the fields openly, unafraid of the enemy
teens.option_d: In the city gate where many could witness
teens.explanation: Judges 6:11 — "Gideon threshed wheat by the winepress, to hide it from the Midianites." Threshing in a winepress (a hollow, concealed space) shows the depth of Israel's fear and oppression.

teens.question: What was Gideon's first act of obedience, and what did he risk by doing it?
teens.option_a: He threw down his father's altar to Baal and cut down the grove — risking his life since the townspeople wanted to kill him
teens.option_b: He gathered an army of thirty-two thousand men
teens.option_c: He offered a sacrifice at the tabernacle at Shiloh
teens.option_d: He tore down Midianite shrines in the valley
teens.explanation: Judges 6:25-30 — God commanded Gideon to throw down Baal's altar. Judges 6:30 — "the men of the city said unto Joash, Bring out thy son, that he may die: because he hath cast down the altar of Baal."

teens.question: What name was Gideon given after he destroyed Baal's altar, and what does it mean?
teens.option_a: Jerubbaal, meaning let Baal plead against him
teens.option_b: Gideon the mighty, meaning warrior of God
teens.option_c: Baal-breaker, meaning destroyer of idols
teens.option_d: El-Gideon, meaning God is my strength
teens.explanation: Judges 6:32 — "Therefore on that day he called him Jerubbaal, saying, Let Baal plead against him, because he hath thrown down his altar."

teens.question: What was the sign that confirmed the angel of the LORD's identity when Gideon offered a meal?
teens.option_a: Fire rose out of the rock and consumed the offering, then the angel vanished
teens.option_b: The angel ate the food and blessed Gideon with visible light
teens.option_c: The offering floated up into the sky
teens.option_d: The bread turned to stone as a permanent memorial
teens.explanation: Judges 6:21 — "Then the angel of the LORD put forth the end of the staff...and there rose up fire out of the rock, and consumed the flesh and the unleavened cakes. Then the angel of the LORD departed out of his sight."

adults.question: What is the theological problem Gideon raises with the prophet's message, and how does it reflect a genuine crisis of faith?
adults.option_a: Gideon contrasts the tradition of God's past acts (Exodus miracles) with present abandonment, asking "where be all his miracles?" — a lament that God's covenant faithfulness seems absent
adults.option_b: He denies that the oppression is punishment for sin
adults.option_c: He challenges the prophet's authority to speak for God
adults.option_d: He argues that Israel had been obedient and did not deserve punishment
adults.explanation: Judges 6:13 — "Oh my Lord, if the LORD be with us, why then is all this befallen us? and where be all his miracles which our fathers told us of?" Gideon's question is a theodicy challenge rooted in the Exodus tradition.

adults.question: How does the divine response to Gideon's objection "my family is poor in Manasseh, and I am the least in my father's house" follow a recurring biblical pattern?
adults.option_a: God chooses the weak and least significant to display His own strength — the pattern of Moses, David, and Paul's "strength in weakness" theology
adults.option_b: God confirms that military rank and family status are irrelevant to divine calling
adults.option_c: God promises to make Gideon's family the greatest in Manasseh
adults.option_d: God corrects Gideon's self-assessment and reveals his true lineage
adults.explanation: Judges 6:15-16 — "I am the least...And the LORD said unto him, Surely I will be with thee." This "least" motif recurs: Moses (Exodus 3:11), Saul (1 Sam. 9:21), David (1 Sam. 16:11), and Paul (1 Cor. 1:27-28).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the name Gideon builds for the altar — Jehovah-shalom — in the context of the military crisis?
adults.option_a: It declares "the LORD is peace" as an act of trust before battle, affirming that true peace comes only from God despite surrounding war and oppression
adults.option_b: It was a peace offering to appease the local Baal deity
adults.option_c: It memorialised the peace treaty Gideon's father made with Midian
adults.option_d: It fulfilled a vow Gideon made to build an altar if Israel prevailed
adults.explanation: Judges 6:24 — "Then Gideon built an altar there unto the LORD, and called it Jehovah-shalom: unto this day it is yet in Ophrah." The name is a confession of trust in God as the source of peace amid the oppression.

adults.question: What is the narrative function of Joash's defense of Gideon in Judges 6:31, and what principle does he invoke?
adults.option_a: Joash argues that if Baal is truly a god he can plead his own cause — a sarcastic challenge that exposes the impotence of idols and defends Gideon's act
adults.option_b: Joash feared the townspeople and so deflected blame from his son
adults.option_c: Joash was secretly a believer in the LORD and saw an opportunity to testify
adults.option_d: Joash invoked the tribal law that a father must defend his son's deeds
adults.explanation: Judges 6:31 — "Will ye plead for Baal? will ye save him? he that will plead for him, let him be put to death...if he be a god, let him plead for himself." Joash's argument mirrors Elijah's challenge to Baal's prophets (1 Kings 18).

adults.question: What does Judges 6:34 say came upon Gideon when he blew the trumpet to gather Israel, and what is the significance of this?
adults.option_a: The Spirit of the LORD came upon Gideon — divine empowerment for leadership, the same pattern seen in the judges Othniel and later Samson
adults.option_b: Gideon was filled with great courage of his own making
adults.option_c: An angel accompanied him visibly as he marched
adults.option_d: A great army appeared miraculously around Gideon
adults.explanation: Judges 6:34 — "But the Spirit of the LORD came upon Gideon, and he blew a trumpet." This Spirit-empowerment formula appears in Judges 3:10 (Othniel), 11:29 (Jephthah), 13:25, 14:6,19 (Samson), marking the judges as Spirit-endowed deliverers.

adults.question: Gideon's fleece test in Judges 6:36-40 involves two requests. What does the progression from first test to second test reveal about the nature of Gideon's faith?
adults.option_a: His faith is growing but still tentative; the reversal of the second test (harder to achieve naturally) shows he genuinely seeks divine confirmation rather than natural coincidence
adults.option_b: Gideon doubted God more after the first sign was given
adults.option_c: The two tests represent two different covenants God was establishing
adults.option_d: The second test was demanded by the elders of Israel, not by Gideon alone
adults.explanation: Judges 6:39 — "Let not thine anger be hot against me...let me prove, I pray thee, but this once." The reversal (dry fleece, wet ground) makes a natural dew explanation impossible, showing Gideon seeking incontrovertible divine confirmation.

adults.question: What does the LORD's instruction in Judges 6:25-26 to use the second bullock of seven years old reveal symbolically?
adults.option_a: The seven-year-old bullock matches the seven years of Midianite oppression, symbolising the reversal of that oppression beginning with the destruction of Baal worship at its source
adults.option_b: The second bull was the largest available and thus the most honourable sacrifice
adults.option_c: Seven years was the legal minimum age for sacrifice in Levitical law
adults.option_d: The bull was Baal's sacred animal and its sacrifice directly challenged Baal's authority
adults.explanation: Judges 6:25 — "Take thy father's young bullock, even the second bullock of seven years old, and throw down the altar of Baal." The seven-year parallel to the period of oppression (Judges 6:1) invites a symbolic reading: the sacrifice begins to reverse the seven years of judgment.

adults.question: What does the sending of a prophet (Judges 6:8-10) before the raising of a deliverer reveal about God's pedagogical method in the book of Judges?
adults.option_a: God first diagnoses the spiritual root of Israel's suffering (covenant violation) through prophetic word before providing military deliverance — addressing cause before symptom
adults.option_b: God always sent two witnesses before acting, following the Mosaic legal requirement
adults.option_c: The prophet was sent to identify which individual would become the next judge
adults.option_d: God used the prophet to announce the end of the Mosaic covenant
adults.explanation: Judges 6:8-10 — The unnamed prophet rehearses the Exodus and covenant, then diagnoses: "ye have not obeyed my voice." God's pattern in Judges: prophetic diagnosis precedes deliverance, so Israel understands WHY they suffered, not merely that they are rescued.

kids.question: How many men did Gideon start with, and how many did God reduce the army to?
kids.explanation: Judges 7:3,7 — Thirty-two thousand men began; the fearful left (22,000), then the water test reduced the army to 300 men.

kids.question: What three things did Gideon's 300 men carry into battle?
kids.option_a: Trumpets, empty pitchers, and lamps inside the pitchers
kids.explanation: Judges 7:16 — "He divided the three hundred men into three companies, and he put a trumpet in every man's hand, with empty pitchers, and lamps within the pitchers."

kids.question: What did Gideon's men shout when they broke their pitchers and blew their trumpets?
kids.option_a: The sword of the LORD and of Gideon
kids.option_d: The LORD our God is one
kids.explanation: Judges 7:20 — "And they cried, The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon."

teens.question: Why did God reduce Gideon's army, according to Judges 7:2?
teens.option_a: Lest Israel vaunt themselves against God, saying their own hand saved them
teens.option_b: So that the battle would be won quickly without a long siege
teens.option_c: To test Gideon's obedience and courage
teens.option_d: Because the Midianite army was small and did not need many soldiers
teens.explanation: Judges 7:2 — "The LORD said unto Gideon, The people that are with thee are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hands, lest Israel vaunt themselves against me, saying, Mine own hand hath saved me."

teens.question: How were the 300 men who remained chosen, and what was the selection criterion at the water?
teens.option_a: Those who lapped water with their tongue like a dog were kept; those who bowed on their knees to drink were sent away
teens.option_b: Those who were not afraid were kept; the fearful were dismissed
teens.option_c: Those from the tribe of Manasseh were kept; others were sent home
teens.option_d: The tallest and strongest three hundred were selected
teens.explanation: Judges 7:5-6 — "Everyone that lappeth of the water with his tongue, as a dog lappeth, him shalt thou set by himself...And the number of them that lapped, putting their hand to their mouth, were three hundred men."

teens.question: What was the dream that a Midianite soldier told his companion, and how was it interpreted?
teens.option_a: A barley cake tumbled into the Midianite camp and overturned a tent — interpreted as the sword of Gideon delivering Midian into his hand
teens.option_b: A great fire swept from the hills and burned the Midianite tents — meaning God would send fire from heaven
teens.option_c: A mighty warrior appeared and threw the camp into the sea — meaning Israel would pursue them to the Jordan
teens.option_d: A trumpet sounded three times and all soldiers fell asleep — meaning God would cause them to sleep through the attack
teens.explanation: Judges 7:13-14 — "A cake of barley bread tumbled into the host of Midian...This is nothing else save the sword of Gideon the son of Joash, a man of Israel: for into his hand hath God delivered Midian."

teens.question: Into how many companies did Gideon divide his 300 men, and when did they blow the trumpets and break the pitchers?
teens.option_a: Three companies; at the beginning of the middle watch, just after the changing of the guard
teens.option_b: Two companies; at midnight when the enemy was deepest asleep
teens.option_c: Four companies; at dawn when the enemy first awoke
teens.option_d: Three companies; at sunset as darkness fell
teens.explanation: Judges 7:16,19 — "He divided the three hundred men into three companies...So Gideon, and the hundred men that were with him, came unto the outside of the camp in the beginning of the middle watch...and they blew the trumpets, and brake the pitchers."

teens.question: What happened among the Midianite soldiers when Gideon's men blew the trumpets?
teens.option_a: The LORD set every man's sword against his fellow throughout all the host and they fled
teens.option_b: The Midianites surrendered immediately upon hearing the trumpets
teens.option_c: Half the Midianites fled to Egypt and the rest were captured
teens.option_d: The Midianites formed ranks but were struck blind by the lamps
teens.explanation: Judges 7:22 — "And the LORD set every man's sword against his fellow, even throughout all the host: and the host fled."

adults.question: What is the theological principle demonstrated by the successive reduction of Gideon's army from 32,000 to 10,000 to 300?
adults.option_a: The extreme reduction ensures that the victory cannot be attributed to human military strength, forcing Israel to credit God alone — a central Deuteronomic principle
adults.option_b: God was testing which soldiers were the most disciplined for unconventional warfare
adults.option_c: God preferred small armies because they were easier to supply
adults.option_d: The reduction fulfilled an ancient prophecy about Manasseh fighting with a handful
adults.explanation: Judges 7:2 states the purpose explicitly: "lest Israel vaunt themselves against me." The reduction makes the human impossibility of victory undeniable, so divine credit is inescapable.

adults.question: What is the most plausible tactical explanation for the water-lapping test, and how does it serve the narrative?
adults.option_a: The lappers remained visually alert (heads up, drinking from cupped hands) while kneeling drinkers were temporarily vulnerable — selecting men with battlefield situational awareness; the narrative uses a mundane test to show divine wisdom in selection
adults.option_b: The lappers were chosen purely by divine arbitrary election with no practical significance
adults.option_c: The test selected men physically stronger than those who knelt
adults.option_d: The test identified men from Gideon's own clan to ensure loyalty
adults.explanation: Judges 7:5-6 — The lapping test is not explained in the text, but the contrast between hand-to-mouth lappers (heads up, hands free) and those who bowed down suggests the former maintained alertness. The text's silence on the reason emphasises divine sovereignty in the selection.

adults.question: What is the significance of the barley cake in the Midianite soldier's dream (Judges 7:13), and why does the interpretation identify it with Gideon?
adults.option_a: Barley was the poor man's grain and the bread of the oppressed; the dream symbol of the lowly barley cake overthrowing the great tent captures the theme of the weak confounding the mighty — the Gideon motif throughout chapter 6
adults.option_b: Barley was Manasseh's tribal crop and thus specifically identified with Gideon
adults.option_c: The barley harvest season was when Gideon began gathering his army
adults.option_d: Barley cakes were used in Midianite divination and thus the dream spoke in their own symbolic language
adults.explanation: Judges 7:13-14 — The barley cake tumbling and overturning a Midianite tent is interpreted as "the sword of Gideon." Barley was the inferior grain (cf. Ruth 2; John 6:9), fitting Gideon's "least of my father's house" status (Judges 6:15).

adults.question: Why did God direct Gideon to eavesdrop on the Midianite camp at night before the battle (Judges 7:9-11), and what psychological purpose did it serve?
adults.option_a: God gave Gideon the option to hear the dream "if thou fear to go down" — it was a mercy provision to strengthen Gideon's confidence before battle, not a military requirement
adults.option_b: God needed Gideon to gather intelligence about Midianite troop positions
adults.option_c: It was a test to see if Gideon would obey an unusual command
adults.option_d: God wanted Gideon to hear Midianite prayers and learn their weakness
adults.explanation: Judges 7:10-11 — "But if thou fear to go down, go thou with Phurah thy servant down to the host: And thou shalt hear what they say; and afterward shall thine hands be strengthened." God diagnoses Gideon's persistent fear and prescribes an encouraging word from the enemy's own mouth.

adults.question: How does the battle tactic of trumpets, pitchers, and lamps (Judges 7:16-20) achieve the desired military effect without conventional weapons?
adults.option_a: The simultaneous explosion of sound (300 trumpets), light (300 lamps), and battle cry from three surrounding directions at night created total disorientation, triggering panic and friendly-fire confusion in the camp — confirmed by Judges 7:22
adults.option_b: The lamps signalled a larger unseen army waiting in the hills
adults.option_c: The trumpets fulfilled the priestly pattern of the walls of Jericho
adults.option_d: The broken pitchers were ceramic containers filled with burning pitch that set the camp alight
adults.explanation: Judges 7:19-22 — The attack at the middle watch (when sentries changed and alertness was lowest), from three directions simultaneously, with 300 trumpet blasts and sudden light, "every man in his place round about the camp." The result: LORD-induced confusion and mutual slaughter (v.22).

adults.question: Gideon sends messengers throughout mount Ephraim to cut off Midianite escape at the Jordan crossings (Judges 7:24). What does this reveal about the scope of tribal cooperation and the battle's second phase?
adults.option_a: The initial 300-man surprise attack is followed by a broader tribal mobilisation, showing the battle had two phases: divine panic and pursuit, requiring Ephraim's cooperation to seal the victory
adults.option_b: Ephraim had already been gathered as a reserve force and was waiting for the signal
adults.option_c: The Ephraimites were angry at being excluded and thus mobilised independently
adults.option_d: Gideon had planned both phases before the battle began
adults.explanation: Judges 7:24-25 — "Gideon sent messengers throughout all mount Ephraim, saying, Come down against the Midianites, and take before them the waters unto Beth-barah and Jordan." The pursuit requires a larger force than 300 to seal escape routes.

adults.question: The Ephraimites capture and kill two Midianite princes named Oreb and Zeeb. What do their names mean, and what is the irony of their fate?
adults.option_a: Oreb means raven and Zeeb means wolf — predatory animals; these princes named as predators are themselves killed at a rock and a winepress, the very sites of Israelite vulnerability and hiding
adults.option_b: Oreb means mighty and Zeeb means swift — warrior epithets that are ironic given their capture
adults.option_c: Oreb means north wind and Zeeb means east wind — directional names indicating their Midianite clans
adults.option_d: Oreb means thorn and Zeeb means lion — common Midianite warrior names without special irony
adults.explanation: Judges 7:25 — Oreb (raven) slain at the rock of Oreb, Zeeb (wolf) at the winepress of Zeeb. The winepress (like Gideon's hiding place in Judges 6:11) is now a site of victory, not fear — the irony of reversal.

adults.question: How does the Gideon narrative in Judges 7 serve as a paradigm for the New Testament principle articulated in 1 Corinthians 1:27-29?
adults.option_a: God chose 300 "foolish" men with pitchers and trumpets to confound 135,000 Midianites (Judges 8:10), directly illustrating Paul's principle that God chooses the weak and foolish to shame the strong, so no flesh may glory
adults.option_b: Paul's principle is about spiritual gifts, not military tactics
adults.option_c: The parallel is coincidental; Paul drew from Greek philosophical tradition, not Judges
adults.option_d: The principle applies only to Paul's church context and cannot be mapped back to Old Testament narratives
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 1:27-29 — "God hath chosen the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty...That no flesh should glory." The Gideon account is a paradigmatic illustration: 300 men, no conventional weapons, total dependence on God, total victory.

kids.question: What did the people of Israel ask Gideon to do after he defeated the Midianites?
kids.option_a: Rule over them as their king
kids.option_c: Lead them to a new land
kids.explanation: Judges 8:22 — "The men of Israel said unto Gideon, Rule thou over us, both thou, and thy son, and thy son's son also: for thou hast delivered us from the hand of Midian."

kids.question: What did Gideon say when the people asked him to be their king?
kids.option_a: I will not rule over you; the LORD shall rule over you
kids.option_b: I will rule over you for forty years
kids.option_c: Let my firstborn son rule instead
kids.option_d: I must pray before I can answer
kids.explanation: Judges 8:23 — "Gideon said unto them, I will not rule over you, neither shall my son rule over you: the LORD shall rule over you."

kids.question: How long did the land have peace during Gideon's lifetime?
kids.explanation: Judges 8:28 — "Thus was Midian subdued before the children of Israel...And the country was in quietness forty years in the days of Gideon."

teens.question: Why were the men of Ephraim angry with Gideon at the beginning of chapter 8?
teens.option_a: He had not called them at the beginning of the fight against Midian
teens.option_b: He had given their land to another tribe
teens.option_c: He had taken Midianite treasure without sharing it
teens.option_d: He had made a treaty with the Midianites without consulting them
teens.explanation: Judges 8:1 — "The men of Ephraim said unto him, Why hast thou served us thus, that thou calledst us not, when thou wentest to fight with the Midianites?"

teens.question: How did Gideon wisely pacify the angry Ephraimites?
teens.option_a: He said their gleanings (capturing the Midianite princes) were better than his vintage, honouring their contribution above his own
teens.option_b: He gave them a share of the Midianite spoil
teens.option_c: He promised to call them first in all future battles
teens.option_d: He invited their elders to be advisors in his household
teens.explanation: Judges 8:2-3 — "Is not the gleaning of the grapes of Ephraim better than the vintage of Abiezer?...Then their anger was abated toward him, when he had said that."

teens.question: What did the men of Succoth and Penuel do when Gideon asked them for bread for his exhausted troops?
teens.option_a: They refused, mocking Gideon by asking if he had already captured Zebah and Zalmunna
teens.option_b: They generously provided food and supplies
teens.option_c: They threatened to alert the Midianites of Gideon's approach
teens.option_d: They demanded payment before giving any bread
teens.explanation: Judges 8:6 — "The princes of Succoth said, Are the hands of Zebah and Zalmunna now in thine hand, that we should give bread unto thine army?"

teens.question: What did Gideon do to the men of Succoth as punishment for refusing to help?
teens.option_a: He tore their flesh with thorns and briers of the wilderness
teens.option_b: He destroyed their city and killed their leaders
teens.option_c: He took their food supply and gave it to others
teens.option_d: He made them servants of the tribe of Manasseh
teens.explanation: Judges 8:16 — "He took the elders of the city, and thorns of the wilderness and briers, and with them he taught the men of Succoth."

teens.question: What spiritual failure did Gideon make after the victory, despite refusing the kingship?
teens.option_a: He made a golden ephod from the spoils and Israel went a-whoring after it as a snare
teens.option_b: He took multiple wives from among the Canaanites
teens.option_c: He stopped worshipping at the tabernacle in Shiloh
teens.option_d: He set up a high place and offered unauthorised sacrifices
teens.explanation: Judges 8:27 — "Gideon made an ephod thereof, and put it in his city, even in Ophrah: and all Israel went thither a-whoring after it: which thing became a snare unto Gideon, and to his house."

adults.question: What rhetorical device does Gideon use to defuse Ephraim's anger in Judges 8:2-3, and what does it reveal about his political intelligence?
adults.option_a: He uses the proverb of gleaning vs. vintage — elevating Ephraim's achievement above his own through self-deprecation — showing mastery of ANE honour culture to prevent intertribal conflict
adults.option_b: He uses a legal argument about the rules of holy war spoil division
adults.option_c: He invokes the Mosaic law about tribal military obligations
adults.option_d: He appeals to the LORD's verdict rather than human honour calculations
adults.explanation: Judges 8:2 — "Is not the gleaning of the grapes of Ephraim better than the vintage of Abiezer?" This viniculture metaphor in an honour-shame society allows Ephraim to feel their contribution was greater, ending the dispute without capitulation.

adults.question: How does Gideon's treatment of Succoth and Penuel (Judges 8:7-9, 15-17) raise a moral tension within the narrative?
adults.option_a: His punishment of fellow Israelites for non-cooperation during the pursuit is severe — thorns and demolishing the tower of Penuel — raising the question of whether his leadership is becoming vindictive and tyrannical
adults.option_b: The text condemns his actions explicitly as sin
adults.option_c: His treatment was legally prescribed by Mosaic law for treason
adults.option_d: The punishment is presented as entirely just with no narrative ambiguity
adults.explanation: Judges 8:7,17 — Gideon threatens then executes thorning of Succoth's elders and demolishing Penuel's tower with its men. The narrator gives no explicit evaluation, creating moral ambiguity characteristic of the Judges' portrayal of flawed deliverers.

adults.question: What does the interrogation and execution of Zebah and Zalmunna in Judges 8:18-21 reveal about Gideon's personal motivation?
adults.option_a: Their killing of Gideon's brothers at Tabor transforms this from holy war into personal blood vengeance — the honour-code demand that he avenge his family
adults.option_b: Gideon killed them purely to fulfil the LORD's command
adults.option_c: He killed them to prevent future Midianite retaliation
adults.option_d: He killed them because the elders of Israel demanded it
adults.explanation: Judges 8:19-20 — "They were my brethren, even the sons of my mother...Then he said unto Jether his firstborn, Up, and slay them." The personal dimension of blood-vengeance intersects with holy war, complicating Gideon's moral portrait.

adults.question: What is the theological irony of the ephod Gideon creates in Judges 8:24-27?
adults.option_a: The man who destroyed Baal's altar creates a religious object that itself becomes an idol causing Israel to go "a-whoring" — his greatest victory is followed by his deepest spiritual failure
adults.option_b: The ephod was a legitimate priestly garment and the problem was only its location in Ophrah
adults.option_c: The ephod was intended as a war memorial and only later became an object of worship
adults.option_d: The text approves of the ephod's creation but condemns its misuse by later generations
adults.explanation: Judges 8:27 — "All Israel went thither a-whoring after it: which thing became a snare unto Gideon, and to his house." The warrior who began by tearing down Baal's altar (Judges 6:25) ends by creating a new object of idolatrous devotion.

adults.question: How many wives and sons did Gideon have, and what does this signal within the Judges narrative about the coming dynastic problem?
adults.option_a: He had many wives and 70 sons, plus a concubine in Shechem who bore Abimelech — the proliferation of heirs directly sets up the fratricidal chaos of Judges 9
adults.option_b: He had one wife and twelve sons representing the twelve tribes
adults.option_c: He had three wives from different tribes to create political alliances
adults.option_d: He had many wives as a sign of blessing, with no negative narrative implication
adults.explanation: Judges 8:30-31 — "Gideon had threescore and ten sons of his body begotten: for he had many wives. And his concubine that was in Shechem, she also bare him a son, whose name he called Abimelech." This genealogy plants the seeds for chapter 9's catastrophe.

adults.question: What does the sudden return to Baal worship after Gideon's death (Judges 8:33-35) reveal about the nature of Israel's covenant loyalty under the judges?
adults.option_a: It was superficial and personality-dependent — tied to the deliverer's presence rather than genuine heart transformation, confirming the cycle's structural inevitability
adults.option_b: Israel was gradually improving in covenant loyalty across the judges period
adults.option_c: Gideon's ephod was the primary cause of the relapse into Baal worship
adults.option_d: The return to Baal was caused by Midianite survivors returning to influence Israel
adults.explanation: Judges 8:33-35 — "As soon as Gideon was dead...the children of Israel turned again, and went a-whoring after Baalim...and remembered not the LORD...Neither shewed they kindness to the house of Jerubbaal." The speed of the relapse ("as soon as") underscores its superficiality.

adults.question: Gideon's refusal of kingship in Judges 8:23 is theologically significant. How does it contrast with Israel's later request to Samuel for a king?
adults.option_a: Gideon's refusal affirms theocracy — "the LORD shall rule over you" — while Samuel's Israel rejects theocracy (1 Sam. 8:7) by demanding a king, marking a progressive deterioration of covenant loyalty
adults.option_b: Gideon refused because he was already functioning as a de facto king in all but name
adults.option_c: The situations are not comparable because Gideon was from Manasseh, not Judah
adults.option_d: Samuel's situation involved a genuine monarchy; Gideon's refusal was only of a tribal chieftainship
adults.explanation: Judges 8:23 — "The LORD shall rule over you." This theocratic principle is the constitutional ideal the judges embody. When Israel demands a king in 1 Samuel 8:7, God interprets it as rejecting divine rule — the same principle Gideon upheld.

adults.question: What is the weight of gold Gideon collected from the Ishmaelite earrings, and what broader cultural pattern does this reflect?
adults.option_a: A thousand and seven hundred shekels of gold, reflecting the ancient Near Eastern practice of dedicating metals from military spoil to religious purposes — but here corrupted into an idolatrous object
adults.option_b: Five hundred shekels, dedicated to the tabernacle as an offering to the LORD
adults.option_c: Three thousand shekels, shared equally among the 300 warriors
adults.option_d: An unstated amount but described as very great, filling a cart
adults.explanation: Judges 8:26 — "The weight of the golden earrings that he requested was a thousand and seven hundred shekels of gold." The dedication of metal spoil to religion mirrors the tabernacle offerings, but here the application becomes a "snare" — an ironic inversion of proper worship.

kids.question: Who was Abimelech's father?
kids.explanation: Judges 9:1 — "Abimelech the son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem unto his mother's brethren." Jerubbaal is Gideon's other name (Judges 8:35).

kids.question: How many of his brothers did Abimelech kill to become ruler?
kids.explanation: Judges 9:5 — "He went unto his father's house at Ophrah, and slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone."

kids.question: How did Abimelech die?
kids.option_a: A woman dropped a millstone on his head
kids.option_b: He was killed by his own soldiers
kids.option_d: He fell off his horse in battle
kids.explanation: Judges 9:53 — "A certain woman cast a piece of a millstone upon Abimelech's head, and all to brake his skull."

teens.question: What parable did Jotham tell to rebuke the men of Shechem for making Abimelech king?
teens.option_a: The parable of the trees seeking a king — the bramble accepted where the olive, fig, and vine all refused
teens.option_b: The parable of the lion and the lamb in the vineyard
teens.option_c: The parable of the sower and the rocky ground
teens.option_d: The parable of the eagle and the cedar of Lebanon
teens.explanation: Judges 9:8-15 — The trees ask the olive, fig, and vine to be king; all refuse because they have productive work. The bramble accepts and threatens to devour the cedars of Lebanon.

teens.question: Who was the one son of Gideon who escaped Abimelech's massacre, and what did he do after warning Shechem?
teens.option_a: Jotham, who escaped by hiding; he delivered his parable from mount Gerizim and then fled to Beer for fear of Abimelech
teens.option_b: Tola, who fled to mount Ephraim and became a judge
teens.option_c: Jair, who escaped to Gilead and raised an army against Abimelech
teens.option_d: Abdon, who hid in a cave and later returned to claim his inheritance
teens.explanation: Judges 9:5 — "Yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left; for he hid himself." Judges 9:7,21 — He proclaimed his parable from Gerizim and "fled, and ran away, and went to Beer."

teens.question: How did God bring judgment on Abimelech and the men of Shechem, according to Judges 9:23-24?
