teens.option_a: God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the men of Shechem so that they dealt treacherously with one another
teens.option_b: God sent a plague upon Shechem that weakened their army
teens.option_c: God raised up enemies from Midian to attack them both
teens.option_d: God caused an earthquake to destroy the tower of Shechem
teens.explanation: Judges 9:23-24 — "God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the men of Shechem; and the men of Shechem dealt treacherously with Abimelech: that the cruelty done to the threescore and ten sons of Jerubbaal might come."

teens.question: Who was Gaal the son of Ebed, and what role did he play in the narrative?
teens.option_a: He stirred up the men of Shechem against Abimelech, claiming they should serve him instead
teens.option_b: He was a Canaanite general who attacked Abimelech from without
teens.option_c: He was the officer of the tower of Shechem who betrayed the city to Abimelech
teens.option_d: He was a prophet who predicted Abimelech's downfall
teens.explanation: Judges 9:28 — "Gaal the son of Ebed said, Who is Abimelech...that we should serve him?" He led the rebellion inside Shechem until Zebul drove him out (Judges 9:40-41).

teens.question: What instruction did Abimelech give his armour-bearer after a woman dropped the millstone on him, and why?
teens.option_a: He told his armour-bearer to thrust him through with his sword, so that men would not say a woman killed him
teens.option_b: He told his armour-bearer to carry him from the battlefield to a physician
teens.option_c: He told his armour-bearer to sound the retreat and negotiate surrender
teens.option_d: He told his armour-bearer to avenge him by killing the woman
teens.explanation: Judges 9:54 — "Abimelech called hastily unto the young man his armourbearer, and said unto him, Draw thy sword, and slay me, that men say not of me, A woman slew him."

adults.question: What is the literary genre of Jotham's speech in Judges 9:7-20, and how does it function within the narrative?
adults.option_a: It is a political fable using an allegory of trees choosing a king, followed by a prophetic curse — the first fable in Scripture, functioning as a legal indictment of Shechem's treachery against Gideon's house
adults.option_b: It is a wisdom poem in the tradition of Proverbs, inserted editorially
adults.option_c: It is a lament over the death of Gideon's sons
adults.option_d: It is a treaty curse formula borrowed from Hittite suzerainty documents
adults.explanation: Judges 9:8-15 is widely identified as the earliest fable in the Bible. It functions as Jotham's legal case: if the men of Shechem acted rightly (v.16,19), good; if not (v.20), let fire devour them — a conditional curse.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the "evil spirit" God sends between Abimelech and Shechem (Judges 9:23)?
adults.option_a: It demonstrates divine retributive justice operating through secondary causes — God does not intervene directly but uses the seeds of treachery already present in Abimelech's violent rule to bring about proportionate punishment
adults.option_b: The evil spirit was a demonic being acting independently of God's will
adults.option_c: It fulfilled a specific Mosaic curse on fratricide
adults.option_d: The evil spirit was a metaphor for political discord with no supernatural content
adults.explanation: Judges 9:23-24 — "God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the men of Shechem...that the cruelty done to the sons of Jerubbaal might come." The narrator explicitly credits God as the agent of retributive justice through providential means.

adults.question: How does the fable's bramble (Judges 9:14-15) serve as a self-commentary on Abimelech's character and rule?
adults.option_a: The bramble is the least noble plant — no fruit, only thorns — and its grandiose self-promotion ("come and put your trust in my shadow") and its threat to burn down the cedars mirrors Abimelech's worthlessness and destructive violence
adults.option_b: The bramble represents the Shechemite people who chose a bad leader
adults.option_c: The bramble is a symbol of Canaanite culture dominating Israelite politics
adults.option_d: The bramble's threat is purely defensive — warning against enemies of Shechem
adults.explanation: Judges 9:14-15 — The bramble alone accepts kingship and immediately threatens destruction. The productive trees (olive, fig, vine) represent Gideon's legitimate sons; the bramble represents the half-Canaanite, fruitless Abimelech.

adults.question: What role does Zebul play in the Abimelech narrative, and what does his character illustrate about political loyalty?
adults.option_a: As governor of Shechem, Zebul secretly alerts Abimelech to Gaal's rebellion, then manipulates Gaal into a military confrontation — illustrating the political informant who serves power regardless of principle
adults.option_b: Zebul is a righteous official who tries to prevent bloodshed between Abimelech and Shechem
adults.option_c: Zebul switches sides from Abimelech to Gaal and back, representing the common people's uncertainty
adults.option_d: Zebul is the commander of Abimelech's army, loyal only because of military rank
adults.explanation: Judges 9:30-33 — Zebul sends secret intelligence to Abimelech and then (Judges 9:36-38) taunts Gaal to fight, engineering a confrontation that destroys Gaal's influence. His motives are entirely political, not theological.

adults.question: How does Jotham's parable in Judges 9:16-20 structure the conditions for its curse, and what double irony is fulfilled in Judges 9:57?
adults.option_a: The curse is conditional on whether Shechem acted "in truth and sincerity"; since they did not, fire from Abimelech should devour Shechem and fire from Shechem devour Abimelech — ironic fulfilment: Abimelech burns the tower of Shechem (v.49) but is then killed at Thebez (v.53)
adults.option_b: The curse was unconditional and applied regardless of Shechem's choices
adults.option_c: The curse was fulfilled when the Midianites destroyed Shechem a generation later
adults.option_d: Jotham's curse was considered legally null because he had no priestly authority
adults.explanation: Judges 9:20 — "Let fire come out from Abimelech, and devour the men of Shechem...and let fire come out from the men of Shechem...and devour Abimelech." Fulfilled: Abimelech burns the tower (v.49, about 1000 die) and is killed at another tower (v.53).

adults.question: What does the detail that Abimelech used seventy pieces of silver from the house of Baal-berith to hire "vain and light persons" (Judges 9:4) reveal about his rule's character?
adults.option_a: Temple silver used to fund a mercenary coup shows the intersection of idolatry and political violence — his kingship is funded by pagan religion and executed by moral nihilists, entirely outside covenantal legitimacy
adults.option_b: The silver amount was a customary payment for tribal leadership transfer
adults.option_c: Baal-berith was a benevolent deity whose silver was appropriate for political use
adults.option_d: The "vain and light persons" were simply young volunteers, not morally suspect individuals
adults.explanation: Judges 9:4 — "They gave him threescore and ten pieces of silver out of the house of Baal-berith, wherewith Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him." The source (pagan temple) and agents (morally worthless men) define his regime from its inception.

adults.question: What is the significance of Abimelech's death at Thebez — killed by a woman with a millstone — within the book's theology of divine justice?
adults.option_a: It fulfils Jotham's curse through ironic reversal: the man who slaughtered 70 on one stone (Judges 9:5) dies by a stone, and the man who suppressed women's honour is shamed by a woman — proportionate, poetic divine retribution
adults.option_b: It was a random accident with no theological significance in the narrative
adults.option_c: It fulfils Deborah's prophecy that military honour belongs to women
adults.option_d: It shows the weakness of Abimelech's siege strategy rather than divine judgment
adults.explanation: Judges 9:5 — sons killed on one stone; Judges 9:53 — Abimelech killed by a millstone. The stone-for-stone symmetry is narratively deliberate. Judges 9:56 explicitly confirms: "God rendered the wickedness of Abimelech...upon his head."

adults.question: How does the Abimelech episode function as a sustained anti-monarchical argument within the book of Judges and the broader Deuteronomic History?
adults.option_a: By showing that illegitimate kingship — born of violence, funded by idolatry, maintained by terror — leads inevitably to fratricidal destruction, it provides a cautionary case study informing the later debates about monarchy in 1 Samuel
adults.option_b: The episode is purely historical with no political theology
adults.option_c: It argues only against half-Canaanite rulers, not against monarchy in general
adults.option_d: The Deuteronomic History favours monarchy and uses Abimelech only as a negative example of the wrong individual, not the institution
adults.explanation: Judges 9 is the only "kingship" episode in Judges before the monarchy. Its catastrophic ending and explicit divine retribution (v.56-57) inform Samuel's warning about kings (1 Sam. 8:11-18) and Jotham's fable remains the clearest anti-monarchical argument in the pre-monarchical corpus.

kids.question: Who judged Israel after Abimelech and was from the tribe of Issachar?
kids.explanation: Judges 10:1 — "After Abimelech there arose to defend Israel Tola the son of Puah, the son of Dodo, a man of Issachar."

kids.question: Jair the Gileadite had thirty sons. What did they ride on?
kids.explanation: Judges 10:4 — "He had thirty sons that rode on thirty ass colts, and they had thirty cities."

kids.question: What did Israel do when they were sorry for worshipping idols, according to Judges 10:16?
kids.option_a: They put away the strange gods from among them and served the LORD
kids.option_b: They offered sacrifices at the tabernacle
kids.option_c: They went to fight their enemies immediately
kids.option_d: They asked a prophet to pray for them
kids.explanation: Judges 10:16 — "They put away the strange gods from among them, and served the LORD: and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel."

teens.question: How many years did Tola judge Israel, and in what city did he dwell?
teens.option_a: Twenty-three years; he dwelt in Shamir in mount Ephraim
teens.option_b: Forty years; he dwelt in Shiloh
teens.option_c: Eighteen years; he dwelt in Bethlehem
teens.option_d: Thirty years; he dwelt in Ramah in Benjamin
teens.explanation: Judges 10:2-3 — "He judged Israel twenty and three years, and died, and was buried in Shamir."

teens.question: How many years did Jair judge Israel, and what was remarkable about the cities his sons controlled?
teens.option_a: Twenty-two years; his thirty sons had thirty cities called Havoth-jair in Gilead
teens.option_b: Forty years; he built thirty new cities in the wilderness
teens.option_c: Fifteen years; his sons governed thirty districts of Canaan
teens.option_d: Thirty years; his sons built the thirty cities of the Levites
teens.explanation: Judges 10:3-4 — "Jair the Gileadite judged Israel twenty and two years...his sons had thirty cities, which are called Havoth-jair unto this day, which are in the land of Gilead."

teens.question: Which peoples oppressed Israel when they again did evil after Jair's death?
teens.option_a: The Philistines and the children of Ammon
teens.option_b: The Midianites and the Moabites
teens.option_c: The Amorites and the Hittites
teens.option_d: The Edomites and the Syrians
teens.explanation: Judges 10:7 — "The anger of the LORD was hot against Israel, and he sold them into the hands of the Philistines, and into the hands of the children of Ammon."

teens.question: How long did the Ammonite and Philistine oppression last before Israel cried to the LORD?
teens.option_a: Eighteen years
teens.option_b: Seven years
teens.option_c: Forty years
teens.option_d: Twenty years
teens.explanation: Judges 10:8 — "That year they vexed and oppressed the children of Israel eighteen years, all the children of Israel that were on the other side Jordan in the land of the Amorites, which is in Gilead."

teens.question: How did the LORD initially respond when Israel cried out for deliverance from Ammonite oppression?
teens.option_a: The LORD refused, recounting all the times He had saved them and been forsaken, and told them to cry to the gods they had chosen
teens.option_b: The LORD immediately raised up Jephthah as a deliverer
teens.option_c: The LORD sent a prophet to give them instructions for repentance
teens.option_d: The LORD sent an angel to tell them to gather their army
teens.explanation: Judges 10:13-14 — "Yet ye have forsaken me, and served other gods: wherefore I will deliver you no more. Go and cry unto the gods which ye have chosen; let them deliver you in the time of your tribulation."

adults.question: How many gods does Judges 10:6 specifically list that Israel served, and what is the significance of the comprehensive list?
adults.option_a: Seven foreign gods: Baalim, Ashtaroth, gods of Syria, Sidon, Moab, Ammon, and Philistia — a comprehensive indictment showing Israel had adopted the idolatry of every surrounding nation
adults.option_b: Three gods: Baal, Asherah, and Molech — the principal Canaanite deities
adults.option_c: Five gods, representing the five Philistine city-states
adults.option_d: Ten gods, one for each of the northern tribes
adults.explanation: Judges 10:6 — "Served Baalim, and Ashtaroth, and the gods of Syria, and the gods of Zidon, and the gods of Moab, and the gods of the children of Ammon, and the gods of the Philistines." The exhaustive list marks the nadir of Israel's apostasy.

adults.question: What is theologically unusual about the LORD's response in Judges 10:13-14, and how does it differ from earlier judge narratives?
adults.option_a: God explicitly refuses to deliver Israel again and sarcastically redirects them to their chosen gods — a direct divine speech of rejection that intensifies the cycle and marks a turning point toward more ambiguous deliverances
adults.option_b: God refuses then immediately relents without any change in Israel's behaviour
adults.option_c: God sends a plague before agreeing to send a deliverer
adults.option_d: God's refusal is temporary and standard, identical to all previous cycles
adults.explanation: Judges 10:13-14 — God's direct speech of refusal ("I will deliver you no more...Go and cry unto the gods which ye have chosen") is unprecedented in the judge narratives. It raises the theological stakes and frames the Jephthah narrative that follows under divine reluctance.

adults.question: What is significant about Judges 10:16's description of Israel putting away foreign gods and the LORD's response that "his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel"?
adults.option_a: The divine pathos expressed — God's soul being grieved or vexed — shows that even after threatening abandonment, God's compassion is moved by genuine repentance, revealing the tension between divine justice and divine mercy
adults.option_b: The phrase is anthropomorphic only and means God had no emotional involvement
adults.option_c: It proves that Israel's repentance was complete and fully accepted
adults.option_d: God's grief is a metaphor for the suffering He would experience in providing a deliverer
adults.explanation: Judges 10:16 — "His soul was grieved for the misery of Israel." The Hebrew naphsho qatsarah (his soul was shortened/vexed) expresses genuine divine compassion despite the preceding statement of refusal — the tension between justice and mercy that characterises the whole book.

adults.question: Tola and Jair are classified as "minor judges." What distinguishes minor judges from major judges in terms of narrative function?
adults.option_a: Minor judges receive no deliverance narrative — only tenure length, lineage, and administrative detail — suggesting they functioned as civil leaders maintaining order rather than as Spirit-empowered military deliverers
adults.option_b: Minor judges were from smaller tribes and therefore received less narrative attention
adults.option_c: Minor judges were illegitimate leaders rejected by some tribes
adults.option_d: The distinction is modern and has no basis in the Hebrew text's presentation
adults.explanation: Tola (Judges 10:1-2) and Jair (Judges 10:3-5) are presented with only genealogical and tenure data, no enemy, no deliverance, no Spirit. This formulaic brevity contrasts with major judge narratives and suggests a different (civil/administrative) function.

adults.question: What geographical scope does Judges 10:8-9 give to the Ammonite oppression, and what does this signal about the crisis's severity?
adults.option_a: The Ammonites oppressed all Israel beyond the Jordan and crossed over to fight Judah, Benjamin, and Ephraim — pan-Israelite scope indicating the most widespread oppression since the book began
adults.option_b: The oppression was limited to the Transjordanian tribes of Gad and Reuben
adults.option_c: Only the tribe of Manasseh was affected because Jephthah was from Gilead
adults.option_d: The Ammonites controlled the coastal plain but did not penetrate the hill country
adults.explanation: Judges 10:8-9 — "They vexed and oppressed...all the children of Israel that were on the other side Jordan...Moreover the children of Ammon passed over Jordan to fight also against Judah, and against Benjamin, and against the house of Ephraim." The breadth creates urgency for a deliverer.

adults.question: How does Israel's confession in Judges 10:10 — "We have sinned against thee, both because we have forsaken our God, and also served Baalim" — compare to earlier repentance statements in Judges?
adults.option_a: It is the most theologically explicit confession in the book: it names the sin precisely (forsaking God and serving Baal) rather than simply crying in distress, yet it follows the same structural pattern of cry after oppression
adults.option_b: It is the first time Israel ever confessed sin in the book of Judges
adults.option_c: It is less sincere than earlier confessions because it comes after eighteen years rather than immediately
adults.option_d: The confession is evaluated positively and immediately accepted by God
adults.explanation: Judges 10:10 — "We have sinned against thee, both because we have forsaken our God, and also served Baalim." Compared to Judges 3:9, 15; 4:3 (simple cries), this confession includes theological self-diagnosis, though God's initial response (Judges 10:13) is still refusal.

adults.question: What does the list of Jair's thirty sons riding thirty ass colts governing thirty cities (Judges 10:4) reveal about the social development of the judge period?
adults.option_a: It indicates a dynastic, hereditary leadership pattern with administrative territorial control — a move toward proto-monarchical governance that anticipates and explains the eventual demand for a king
adults.option_b: It was a sign of divine blessing with no political implications
adults.option_c: The thirty cities were all Levitical cities assigned for priestly administration
adults.option_d: The ass colts were military mounts showing Jair maintained a cavalry force
adults.explanation: Judges 10:4 — The thirty sons each governing a named city (Havoth-jair) with their own mount (a status symbol, cf. Judges 5:10; 12:14) reveals administrative infrastructure more associated with kingship than with the Spirit-called deliverers of the major judge narratives.

adults.question: How does Judges 10 serve as the theological hinge between the Abimelech episode and the Jephthah narrative, and what does this structural placement accomplish?
adults.option_a: It resets the Deuteronomic cycle after the anomalous Abimelech anti-kingship episode, escalates the apostasy to an all-time catalogue of seven gods, introduces the most severe divine refusal yet, then establishes the desperation that will make Jephthah's flawed leadership understandable
adults.option_b: It serves as a purely transitional chapter with no theological content of its own
adults.option_c: It introduces the Philistine threat that will dominate Samson's narrative
adults.option_d: It explains why the Ammonites and Philistines were simultaneously powerful enough to double-oppress Israel
adults.explanation: Judges 10 functions as a theological escalation point: the seven-god apostasy (v.6) exceeds all previous sin catalogs; the divine refusal (v.13-14) is unprecedented; the genuine repentance (v.16) and divine compassion introduce Jephthah's ambiguous deliverance in chapters 11-12 under conditions of maximum crisis.

kids.question: What was Jephthah known as because of his mother?
kids.explanation: Judges 11:1 — "Now Jephthah the Gileadite was a mighty man of valour, and he was the son of an harlot."

kids.question: Which nation did Jephthah fight against to save Israel?
kids.explanation: Judges 11:32 — "So Jephthah passed over unto the children of Ammon to fight against them; and the LORD delivered them into his hands."

kids.question: What rash vow did Jephthah make before going into battle?
kids.option_a: He would offer whatever came out of his house first as a burnt offering
kids.option_b: He would fast for forty days
kids.option_c: He would build an altar to God
kids.option_d: He would give half his wealth to the priests
kids.explanation: Judges 11:31 — "then it shall be, that whatsoever cometh forth of the doors of my house to meet me...I will offer it up for a burnt offering."

teens.question: Why did Jephthah's half-brothers drive him away from his home?
teens.option_a: He was the son of a strange woman and they would not share their father's inheritance with him
teens.option_b: He stole their cattle
teens.option_c: He allied with Israel's enemies
teens.option_d: He refused to worship at the tabernacle
teens.explanation: Judges 11:2 — "And Gilead's wife bare him sons; and his wife's sons grew up, and they thrust out Jephthah, and said unto him, Thou shalt not inherit in our father's house; for thou art the son of a strange woman."

teens.question: What did Jephthah argue to the king of Ammon about who rightfully owned the disputed territory?
teens.option_a: Israel took the land from the Amorites, not from Ammon, so it belonged to Israel
teens.option_b: Abraham had purchased the land centuries before
teens.option_c: God gave the land directly to Gilead by name
teens.option_d: The land was uninhabited when Israel arrived
teens.explanation: Judges 11:23-24 — "So now the LORD God of Israel hath dispossessed the Amorites from before his people Israel, and shouldest thou possess it?" Jephthah argued the land came from the Amorites, not Ammon.

teens.question: Who first came out of Jephthah's house to meet him when he returned from victory?
teens.option_a: His daughter, his only child
teens.option_b: His wife
teens.option_c: His servants
teens.option_d: A group of young men
teens.explanation: Judges 11:34 — "And Jephthah came to Mizpeh unto his house, and, behold, his daughter came out to meet him with timbrels and with dances: and she was his only child."

teens.question: What did Jephthah's daughter request before her fate was sealed?
teens.option_a: Two months to bewail her virginity in the mountains
teens.option_b: Permission to go to the tabernacle to pray
teens.option_c: A chance to say farewell to all the tribes of Israel
teens.option_d: That her father would not make the vow
teens.explanation: Judges 11:37 — "And she said unto her father, Let this thing be done for me: let me alone two months, that I may go up and down upon the mountains, and bewail my virginity."

teens.question: What became a custom in Israel following the events concerning Jephthah's daughter?
teens.option_a: The daughters of Israel went four days each year to lament
teens.option_b: Israel banned rash vows
teens.option_c: The tabernacle held an annual fast in her memory
teens.option_d: Young women were forbidden from battle sendoffs
teens.explanation: Judges 11:40 — "And it was a custom in Israel, That the daughters of Israel went yearly to lament the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite four days in a year."

adults.question: What does the land of Tob, where Jephthah fled, represent in his story?
adults.option_a: A pagan territory where rejected outcasts could find community outside Israel's covenant structure
adults.option_b: A priestly city of refuge
adults.option_c: A Canaanite stronghold he helped defend
adults.option_d: A place of divine training like Moses in Midian
adults.explanation: Judges 11:3 — "Then Jephthah fled from his brethren, and dwelt in the land of Tob: and there were gathered vain men to Jephthah, and went out with him." Tob lay in the Transjordan frontier, outside normal Israelite governance.

adults.question: How many years does Jephthah's historical argument in his embassy span, and what does this imply about Israel's claim?
adults.option_a: Three hundred years, showing an ancient and well-established Israelite possession
adults.option_b: Forty years, matching the wilderness generation
adults.option_c: One hundred years, spanning Saul to David
adults.option_d: Seventy years, the span of a generation
adults.explanation: Judges 11:26 — "While Israel dwelt in Heshbon and her towns...three hundred years? why therefore did ye not recover them within that time?" A three-century uncontested possession was a powerful legal and historical argument.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the Spirit of the LORD coming upon Jephthah in verse 29?
adults.option_a: It validates him despite his social illegitimacy, showing God's gifts are not bound by human social structures
adults.option_b: It guarantees absolute success without any further conditions
adults.option_c: It means Jephthah became a prophet as well as a judge
adults.option_d: It cancels his rash vow retrospectively
adults.explanation: Judges 11:29 — "Then the Spirit of the LORD came upon Jephthah." God's Spirit coming on a social outcast parallels Gideon and Samson, showing grace chooses the unlikely.

adults.question: How does Jephthah's reference to "Chemosh your god" in verse 24 function in his diplomatic argument?
adults.option_a: He appears to grant the existence of Chemosh to make a comparative legal argument, not affirm pagan theology
adults.option_b: He worships Chemosh alongside the LORD
adults.option_c: He claims Chemosh has given the land to Israel
adults.option_d: He uses it as a curse against Ammon
adults.explanation: Judges 11:24 — "Wilt not thou possess that which Chemosh thy god giveth thee to possess?" Jephthah uses a comparative legal argument: just as Ammon accepts what its god gives, Israel has right to what the LORD gave Israel.

adults.question: What is the dominant scholarly debate regarding the fate of Jephthah's daughter?
adults.option_a: Whether she was literally offered as a burnt sacrifice or consecrated to perpetual virginity at the tabernacle
adults.option_b: Whether the story is historical or purely allegorical
adults.option_c: Whether Jephthah was a legitimate judge of Israel
adults.option_d: Whether her mother is identified elsewhere in Scripture
adults.explanation: Judges 11:39 — "he did with her according to his vow...and she knew no man." The phrase "knew no man" leads many scholars to argue for perpetual virginity rather than death, while others insist the burnt-offering language demands literal interpretation.

adults.question: What does the book of Hebrews say about Jephthah that complicates simple moral condemnation of him?
adults.option_a: Hebrews 11:32 lists Jephthah among heroes of faith alongside Gideon, Barak, and Samson
adults.option_b: Hebrews warns Jephthah is an example of rash prayer
adults.option_c: Hebrews says Jephthah's vow was later annulled by a priest
adults.option_d: Hebrews does not mention Jephthah at all
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:32 — "And what shall I more say? for the time would fail me to tell of Gedeon, and of Barak, and of Samson, and of Jephthae." His listing among faith heroes shows God credits overall faith even when a servant makes grave errors.

adults.question: What does Jephthah's negotiated return from Tob tell us about covenant community and pragmatic leadership in ancient Israel?
adults.option_a: The elders of Gilead were willing to restore an outcast as head when military crisis demanded it, showing authority was conditional and pragmatic
adults.option_b: Israel had no formal mechanism for restoring exiles
adults.option_c: Jephthah demanded unconditional reinstatement before fighting
adults.option_d: The priesthood approved Jephthah's return through divination
adults.explanation: Judges 11:8-9 — "And the elders of Gilead said unto Jephthah...Therefore we turn again to thee now...And Jephthah said unto the elders of Gilead, If ye bring me home again to fight...then I will be your head." The negotiation reflects a contractual view of leadership.

adults.question: What does Jephthah's invocation of the LORD as judge in verse 27 reveal about his theology?
adults.option_a: He held a high view of God as impartial arbiter between nations, a divine judge above both Ammon and Israel
adults.option_b: He believed God only judged Israel and ignored the nations
adults.option_c: He was appealing to international arbitration treaties
adults.option_d: He doubted the LORD's power and sought a human judge instead
adults.explanation: Judges 11:27 — "Wherefore I have not sinned against thee, but thou doest me wrong to war against me: the LORD the Judge be judge this day between the children of Israel and the children of Ammon." This is a remarkable invocation of God as universal judge.

kids.question: What tribe quarrelled with Jephthah after the battle against Ammon?
kids.explanation: Judges 12:1 — "And the men of Ephraim gathered themselves together...and said unto Jephthah, Wherefore passedst thou over to fight against the children of Ammon?"

kids.question: What word could the Ephraimites not pronounce correctly at the fords of Jordan?
kids.explanation: Judges 12:6 — "Then said they unto him, Say now Shibboleth: and he said Sibboleth: for he could not frame to pronounce it right."

kids.question: How many Ephraimites were slain at the fords of Jordan?
kids.explanation: Judges 12:6 — "And there fell at that time of the Ephraimites forty and two thousand."

teens.question: What did Ephraim threaten to do to Jephthah and his house?
teens.option_a: Burn his house down upon him
teens.option_b: Take him captive to Shiloh
teens.option_c: Banish him from Israel
teens.option_d: Kill all of Gilead's livestock
teens.explanation: Judges 12:1 — "we will burn thine house upon thee with fire." The Ephraimites threatened arson against Jephthah's household.

teens.question: How did Jephthah counter Ephraim's complaint that he had not called them to fight?
teens.option_a: He said he had called them but they did not save him, so he risked his life and the LORD delivered Ammon to him
teens.option_b: He said Ephraim was too far away to summon in time
teens.option_c: He said God had forbidden him to call for reinforcements
teens.option_d: He acknowledged his error and made peace with them
teens.explanation: Judges 12:2-3 — "I and my people were at great strife with the children of Ammon; and when I called you, ye delivered me not out of their hands. And when I saw that ye delivered me not, I put my life in my hands."

teens.question: How many years did Jephthah judge Israel?
teens.option_a: Six years
teens.option_b: Eight years
teens.option_c: Twenty years
teens.option_d: Forty years
teens.explanation: Judges 12:7 — "And Jephthah judged Israel six years. Then died Jephthah the Gileadite, and was buried in the cities of Gilead."

teens.question: Which of the minor judges after Jephthah had thirty sons and thirty daughters whom he gave in marriage, and thirty sons riding on thirty donkeys?
teens.option_a: Ibzan of Bethlehem
teens.option_b: Elon the Zebulonite
teens.option_c: Abdon the Pirathonite
teens.option_d: Jair the Gileadite
teens.explanation: Judges 12:8-9 — "And after him Ibzan of Beth-lehem judged Israel. And he had thirty sons, and thirty daughters, whom he sent abroad, and took in thirty daughters from abroad for his sons."

teens.question: How many sons and grandsons did Abdon the Pirathonite have, and what did they ride?
teens.option_a: Forty sons and thirty nephews riding on seventy ass colts
teens.option_b: Twenty sons and twenty grandsons riding on donkeys
teens.option_c: Thirty sons and thirty grandsons riding on horses
teens.option_d: Forty sons and thirty grandsons riding on camels
teens.explanation: Judges 12:14 — "And he had forty sons and thirty nephews, that rode on threescore and ten ass colts: and he judged Israel eight years."

adults.question: What does the Shibboleth episode reveal about dialect variation in ancient tribal Israel?
adults.option_a: Different tribes of Israel spoke recognisably distinct dialects, such that a single phoneme could betray tribal identity
adults.option_b: All Israelites spoke one unified dialect and only Ephraimites had a foreign accent
adults.option_c: The Ephraimites were actually Canaanites pretending to be Israelites
adults.option_d: The test was based on a foreign language unknown to most Israelites
adults.explanation: Judges 12:6 — "for he could not frame to pronounce it right." The inability to produce the /sh/ sound in "Shibboleth" reflects genuine phonological variation across Israelite tribal dialects.

adults.question: What does Ephraim's repeated complaint (also seen in Judges 8 with Gideon) reveal about intertribal politics in the period of the judges?
adults.option_a: Ephraim had a persistent sense of tribal superiority and expected to be included in major military campaigns
adults.option_b: Ephraim was legally required to lead every battle
adults.option_c: Ephraim was the weakest tribe and sought glory it could not win militarily
adults.option_d: Ephraim's complaints were always justified and acknowledged by the judges
adults.explanation: Judges 12:1 — "Why passedst thou over to fight against the children of Ammon, and didst not call us to go with thee?" In Judges 8:1 Ephraim made the same complaint to Gideon. Gideon appeased them; Jephthah did not, resulting in civil war.

adults.question: How does Jephthah's handling of the Ephraimite challenge contrast with Gideon's in Judges 8, and what does this tell us about leadership?
adults.option_a: Gideon used a soft answer to turn away wrath; Jephthah's blunt refusal of blame escalated to civil war, showing how a leader's temperament shapes communal outcomes
adults.option_b: Jephthah and Gideon both went to war against Ephraim
adults.option_c: Gideon also fought Ephraim but it is not recorded
adults.option_d: Jephthah was righteous and Gideon was wrong to appease Ephraim
adults.explanation: Judges 8:3 — Gideon's "soft answer" satisfied Ephraim. Jephthah's confrontational reply in Judges 12:2-3 led to 42,000 deaths. The contrast illustrates Proverbs 15:1: "A soft answer turneth away wrath."

adults.question: What is the lexical meaning of "Shibboleth" and what two meanings has it carried in subsequent history?
adults.option_a: An ear of grain or a flowing stream; later used to mean any test of belonging or linguistic marker of group identity
adults.option_b: A battle cry meaning "victory to the LORD"; later used to mean any religious oath
adults.option_c: A type of weapon; later used to mean a military password
adults.option_d: A Canaanite word for idol; later used to mean any pagan practice
adults.explanation: Judges 12:6 — "Say now Shibboleth." The Hebrew word means both "ear of grain" and "flowing stream." Historically, it has given English the word "shibboleth" — a linguistic or cultural test used to identify insiders and outsiders.

adults.question: What is the theological irony of Ephraim, whose ancestor received the greater blessing from Jacob (Gen. 48), being decimated in a civil war within Israel?
adults.option_a: The tribe that inherited the patriarchal blessing of preeminence was brought low by pride and internecine strife
adults.option_b: Ephraim had forfeited its blessing by worshipping Canaanite idols
adults.option_c: The decimation of Ephraim fulfilled a specific prophecy
adults.option_d: Ephraim was being punished for the sins of Joseph
adults.explanation: Genesis 48:19-20 gives Ephraim the greater blessing over Manasseh. Judges 12:6 records 42,000 Ephraimites slain by fellow Israelites, a tragic fulfillment of the warning that pride goes before a fall (Proverbs 16:18).

adults.question: What does the rapid succession of minor judges (Ibzan, Elon, Abdon) in a single chapter suggest about the narrative structure of Judges?
adults.option_a: The author is accelerating the narrative to reach the Samson cycle, using minor judges as transitional material
adults.option_b: These judges were more important than the major judges
adults.option_c: The minor judges indicate a period of total spiritual revival
adults.option_d: Each minor judge ruled a different region with no overlap
adults.explanation: Judges 12:8-15 compresses three judgeships into eight verses. The formulaic treatment contrasts sharply with the extended Jephthah and Samson narratives, serving as a literary bridge.

adults.question: What does the detail that Ibzan sent his thirty daughters abroad and brought in thirty foreign daughters for his sons suggest about marriage practices in this period?
adults.option_a: Exogamous marriage alliances were used politically by leaders to build cross-tribal coalitions
adults.option_b: Ibzan was disobeying the Mosaic law forbidding intermarriage with Canaanites
adults.option_c: The daughters were sent as tribute to foreign kings
adults.option_d: This was a normal religious requirement for judges of Israel
adults.explanation: Judges 12:9 — "he sent abroad, and took in thirty daughters from abroad for his sons." Whether these marriages were within Israel or outside it is debated, but the pattern reflects the use of marriage for political alliance-building common in the ancient Near East.

adults.question: What does the cumulative toll of internecine bloodshed in Judges 12 (42,000 dead) reveal about the spiritual state of Israel at this time?
adults.option_a: Israel had so thoroughly abandoned covenant unity that the tribes were willing to slaughter one another, accelerating the very chaos the judges were meant to prevent
adults.option_b: The civil war was a necessary purification of Israel
adults.option_c: God had decreed the civil war as punishment for Ephraim's idolatry
adults.option_d: The 42,000 figure is symbolic and should not be read literally
adults.explanation: Judges 12:6 — "there fell at that time of the Ephraimites forty and two thousand." Internecine killing on this scale contrasts starkly with the covenant ideal of Israel as one people, illustrating the downward spiral the book of Judges traces.

kids.question: Who was Samson's father?
kids.explanation: Judges 13:2 — "And there was a certain man of Zorah, of the family of the Danites, whose name was Manoah."

kids.question: What appeared to Manoah's wife to announce the birth of Samson?
kids.explanation: Judges 13:3 — "And the angel of the LORD appeared unto the woman, and said unto her, Behold now, thou art barren, and bearest not: but thou shalt conceive, and bear a son."

kids.question: What special rule was the unborn Samson to follow from birth?
kids.option_a: No razor shall come upon his head, for he shall be a Nazirite
kids.option_c: He must pray three times daily
kids.option_d: He must live in the tabernacle
kids.explanation: Judges 13:5 — "no razor shall come on his head: for the child shall be a Nazarite unto God from the womb."

teens.question: What three things was Manoah's wife told to avoid during her pregnancy?
teens.option_a: Wine, strong drink, and all unclean things
teens.option_b: Meat, leaven, and contact with the dead
teens.option_c: Idols, incense, and pagan feasts
teens.option_d: Salt, oil, and foreign grain
teens.explanation: Judges 13:4 — "Now therefore beware, I pray thee, and drink not wine nor strong drink, and eat not any unclean thing."

teens.question: What did Manoah offer the angel, and how did the angel instruct him to present it?
teens.option_a: A kid goat; offer it as a burnt offering to the LORD rather than to himself
teens.option_b: A lamb; sacrifice it at the tabernacle first
teens.option_c: Bread and wine; pour them on the altar
teens.option_d: An ox; give it to the priests of Shiloh
teens.explanation: Judges 13:16 — "And the angel of the LORD said unto Manoah...offer it unto the LORD." The angel refused to eat food meant for him but directed it be offered to God.

teens.question: What miraculous sign occurred when Manoah offered the sacrifice on the rock?
teens.option_a: The flame ascended toward heaven and the angel ascended in the flame
teens.option_b: Fire came down from heaven and consumed the altar
teens.option_c: A rainbow appeared in the sky
teens.option_d: The rock split open and water flowed out
teens.explanation: Judges 13:20 — "For it came to pass, when the flame went up toward heaven from off the altar, that the angel of the LORD ascended in the flame of the altar."

teens.question: Why was Manoah afraid after the angel ascended in the flame?
teens.option_a: He thought he and his wife would surely die because they had seen God
teens.option_b: He feared the Philistines had seen the miracle
teens.option_c: He worried the vow was invalid because the angel had left
teens.option_d: He thought the sacrifice had been rejected
teens.explanation: Judges 13:22 — "And Manoah said unto his wife, We shall surely die, because we have seen God." His fear reflects the ancient belief that no man could see God and live.

teens.question: What name was given to the angel when Manoah asked for it?
teens.option_a: The angel said his name was secret (wonderful)
teens.option_b: The angel said his name was Gabriel
teens.option_c: The angel refused to give any name at all
teens.option_d: The angel said his name was Michael
teens.explanation: Judges 13:18 — "And the angel of the LORD said unto him, Why askest thou thus after my name, seeing it is secret?" The Hebrew word translated "secret" is also rendered "wonderful" in some versions.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Manoah's wife being described as barren before the angelic announcement?
adults.option_a: It places Samson's birth in the pattern of miraculous divine provision like Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, and later Samuel and John the Baptist
adults.option_b: It means Samson was not truly human but had a divine nature
adults.option_c: It proves Manoah was being punished for a secret sin
adults.option_d: It is simply a medical detail with no theological significance
adults.explanation: Judges 13:2-3 — "his wife was barren...Behold now, thou art barren...but thou shalt conceive." Barren mothers who bear deliverers is a recurring biblical motif: Sarah (Gen. 18), Rachel (Gen. 30), Hannah (1 Sam. 1), Elizabeth (Luke 1).

adults.question: What does the description of the angel as "very terrible" (awesome) in verse 6 indicate about the nature of the visitor?
adults.option_a: The appearance of the angel had overwhelming divine majesty that inspired reverential awe, suggesting a theophany rather than an ordinary angelic visit
adults.option_b: The angel was physically frightening in a demonic sense
adults.option_c: The woman was simply afraid of strangers
adults.option_d: The angel was wearing battle armour
adults.explanation: Judges 13:6 — "a man of God came unto me, and his countenance was like the countenance of an angel of God, very terrible." The combination with the secret name and the flame ascent in verse 20 has led many interpreters to identify this as the Angel of the LORD — a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ.

adults.question: How does the Nazirite vow prescribed for Samson in Judges 13 differ from the voluntary Nazirite vow described in Numbers 6?
adults.option_a: Samson's vow was lifelong and imposed from the womb by divine decree; Numbers 6 describes a voluntary, temporary vow that an individual chooses
adults.option_b: Samson's vow was identical to Numbers 6 in every respect
adults.option_c: Numbers 6 also permits lifelong Nazirites from birth
adults.option_d: Samson's vow was given by a priest, not an angel
adults.explanation: Judges 13:5 — "the child shall be a Nazarite unto God from the womb to the day of his death." Numbers 6:2-5 describes an individual voluntarily consecrating themselves for a defined period.

adults.question: What does the angel's refusal to eat food in Judges 13:16 suggest about the nature of the messenger?
adults.option_a: It indicates a transcendent, non-corporeal being not requiring sustenance — contrasting with angels in Genesis 18-19 who ate with Abraham and Lot
adults.option_b: Angels always refuse human food in the Bible
adults.option_c: The angel was following a fast as part of his mission
adults.option_d: It was a test of Manoah's generosity
adults.explanation: Judges 13:16 — "And the angel of the LORD said unto Manoah, Though thou detain me, I will not eat of thy bread." Genesis 18:8 depicts angels eating with Abraham. The contrast raises deep questions about angelology and theophany in the Hebrew Bible.

adults.question: What is the narrative irony of Israel being oppressed by the Philistines when Samson is announced as deliverer in Judges 13:5?
adults.option_a: The announcement declares "he shall begin to deliver Israel," implying Samson's deliverance will be incomplete — a deliberate narrative foreshadowing of the paradoxical and flawed nature of his judgeship
adults.option_b: The Philistine oppression had already ended when Samson was born
adults.option_c: Samson was destined to fully and finally defeat Philistia
adults.option_d: The word "begin" means "completely accomplish" in Hebrew
adults.explanation: Judges 13:5 — "he shall begin to deliver Israel out of the hand of the Philistines." The word "begin" is significant — Israel was not fully delivered from Philistia until Samuel's time (1 Sam. 7), showing Samson only initiates a longer process.

adults.question: What does Manoah's wife's reassurance to her husband in verses 22-23 reveal about her theological insight compared to his?
adults.option_a: She displayed greater theological reasoning: God would not have accepted their sacrifice and shown them these things if He intended to kill them
adults.option_b: She was simply trying to calm his emotions without theological substance
adults.option_c: Manoah was right to be afraid and her reassurance was naive
adults.option_d: Her argument was borrowed from pagan religious reasoning
adults.explanation: Judges 13:23 — "But his wife said unto him, If the LORD were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering and a meat offering at our hands." Her argument from divine consistency and grace is theologically astute.

adults.question: How does Judges 13 function as a literary counterpart to Judges 16, and what does the contrast reveal?
adults.option_a: Chapter 13 shows Samson's life beginning with divine glory — miraculous birth, angelic announcement, Nazirite consecration; chapter 16 shows the squandering of that gift through sin, reversing every element
adults.option_b: Chapter 13 focuses on his mother; chapter 16 focuses on his father
adults.option_c: Both chapters show Samson at his spiritual best
adults.option_d: The two chapters are from different literary sources with no intended connection
adults.explanation: Judges 13:24-25 — "And the woman bare a son, and called his name Samson: and the child grew, and the LORD blessed him. And the Spirit of the LORD began to move him." The glorious beginning makes the catastrophic end of chapter 16 all the more tragic.

adults.question: What does the phrase "the Spirit of the LORD began to move him at times in the camp of Dan" in verse 25 suggest about the nature of Samson's supernatural strength?
adults.option_a: It was episodic and Spirit-given, not an intrinsic physical quality — implying his strength was always a divine gift that could be withdrawn
adults.option_b: He was naturally the strongest man alive and the Spirit enhanced that strength
adults.option_c: The Spirit moved him only in the tribe of Dan and nowhere else
adults.option_d: The movement of the Spirit referred to prophetic visions, not physical strength
adults.explanation: Judges 13:25 — "And the Spirit of the LORD began to move him at times." The phrase "at times" and "began to move" indicates intermittent, Spirit-driven empowerment, which makes his later loss of strength when the Spirit departs (Judges 16:20) theologically coherent.

kids.question: What animal did Samson kill with his bare hands on the way to Timnath?
kids.explanation: Judges 14:6 — "And he rent him as he would have rent a kid, and he had nothing in his hand."

kids.question: What did Samson find inside the dead lion when he returned?
kids.option_a: A swarm of bees and honey
kids.explanation: Judges 14:8 — "And after a time he returned to take her, and he turned aside to see the carcase of the lion: and, behold, there was a swarm of bees and honey in the carcase of the lion."

kids.question: What was the prize for solving Samson's riddle at the wedding feast?
kids.option_a: Thirty sheets and thirty change of garments
kids.explanation: Judges 14:12 — "I will now put forth a riddle unto you...if ye can certainly declare it me within the seven days of the feast, then I will give you thirty sheets and thirty change of garments."

teens.question: What was Samson's riddle at the wedding feast?
teens.option_a: "Out of the eater came forth meat, and out of the strong came forth sweetness"
teens.option_b: "A lion ate honey yet lived; how can this be?"
teens.option_c: "That which I killed gave me life; that which was dead gave sweetness"
teens.option_d: "Strong and sweet in the same house; what is it?"
teens.explanation: Judges 14:14 — "And he said unto them, Out of the eater came forth meat, and out of the strong came forth sweetness. And they could not in three days expound the riddle."

teens.question: How did the Philistine companions persuade Samson's wife to get the answer to the riddle?
teens.option_a: They threatened to burn her and her father's house with fire
teens.option_b: They offered her silver and gold
teens.option_c: They promised to make her husband king
teens.option_d: They threatened to report her father to the Philistine rulers
teens.explanation: Judges 14:15 — "And it came to pass on the seventh day, that they said unto Samson's wife, Entice thy husband, that he may declare unto us the riddle, lest we burn thee and thy father's house with fire."

teens.question: Where did Samson go to get the thirty changes of garments to pay his debt after they answered the riddle?
teens.option_a: He went to Ashkelon and slew thirty Philistines and took their spoil
teens.option_b: He went to Shiloh and took temple garments
teens.option_c: He went back to his father's house to collect them
teens.option_d: He demanded them from the wedding guests
teens.explanation: Judges 14:19 — "And the Spirit of the LORD came upon him, and he went down to Ashkelon, and slew thirty men of them, and took their spoil."

teens.question: What happened to Samson's Philistine wife after he departed in anger?
teens.option_a: She was given to his companion who had been his best man
teens.option_b: She was sent back to her father's house alone
teens.option_c: She fled to Israel to find Samson
teens.option_d: She was executed for revealing the riddle
teens.explanation: Judges 14:20 — "But Samson's wife was given to his companion, whom he had used as his friend." This betrayal became the seed of further conflict in chapter 15.

teens.question: What was God's hidden purpose behind Samson's desire to marry a Philistine woman?
teens.option_a: God was seeking an occasion against the Philistines
teens.option_b: God was testing Samson's faithfulness
teens.option_c: God wanted Samson to convert the Philistines
teens.option_d: God was fulfilling a covenant with the tribe of Dan
teens.explanation: Judges 14:4 — "But his father and his mother knew not that it was of the LORD, that he sought an occasion against the Philistines." God used even Samson's fleshly desires as part of His sovereign plan.

adults.question: What Nazirite violation is implied by Samson eating honey from the lion's carcass, and what does this signal about his character?
adults.option_a: Contact with a dead animal carcass violated the Nazirite prohibition against touching anything dead (Numbers 6:6), revealing that Samson treated his consecration casually from early on
adults.option_b: There is no Nazirite prohibition on honey from animals
adults.option_c: The violation was forgiven because the honey was a gift from God
adults.option_d: The lion was not technically dead at the moment Samson ate
adults.explanation: Numbers 6:6 — "he shall come at no dead body." Judges 14:8-9 — Samson scooped honey from the lion's carcass. This early, casual violation of his Nazirite vow foreshadows the tragic end of chapter 16.

adults.question: What does the phrase "of the LORD" in Judges 14:4 introduce theologically, and how does it relate to the doctrine of divine providence?
adults.option_a: It affirms that God can direct even sinful human desires toward His sovereign purposes without becoming the author of sin
adults.option_b: It means God approved of marriage to Canaanite women
adults.option_c: It means Samson was sinlessly obeying God by marrying a Philistine
adults.option_d: It is a later editorial addition attempting to excuse Samson's sin
adults.explanation: Judges 14:4 — "it was of the LORD, that he sought an occasion against the Philistines." This is a key text for divine providence — God works through human passions and sinful choices without endorsing them, steering history toward His purposes.

adults.question: What does the structure of Samson's riddle — "eater/meat" and "strong/sweetness" — and his later explanation in Judges 14:18 reveal about his emotional state?
adults.option_a: His counter-riddle "If ye had not plowed with my heifer" reveals deep personal betrayal — he experienced intimacy as violation, likening his wife to a farm animal exploited by others
adults.option_b: His explanation was purely intellectual with no emotional content
adults.option_c: His counter-riddle was a standard Philistine proverb he quoted
adults.option_d: He was proud of solving the riddle himself and boasted of it
adults.explanation: Judges 14:18 — "If ye had not plowed with my heifer, ye had not found out my riddle." The agricultural metaphor for sexual betrayal (heifer/plowing) is a raw expression of betrayal rather than a compliment to his wife.

adults.question: How does Judges 14:19 present the Spirit of the LORD and violence together, and what theological tension does this create?
adults.option_a: The Spirit empowers Samson to kill thirty men — raising the same tension as holy war texts: God's Spirit authorising lethal violence as judicial punishment on Israel's oppressors
adults.option_b: The Spirit only moved Samson spiritually and had nothing to do with the killing
adults.option_c: God condemned the killing even while the Spirit moved him
adults.option_d: The violence was purely Samson's own sin and the Spirit had already withdrawn
adults.explanation: Judges 14:19 — "And the Spirit of the LORD came upon him, and he went down to Ashkelon, and slew thirty men." The Spirit's involvement in violent deliverance connects to holy war theology in Deuteronomy 20 and the conquest narratives.

adults.question: What does Samson's parents' objection to a Philistine marriage (v. 3) and his insistence reveal about the tension between parental authority and individual desire in the Mosaic law?
adults.option_a: Deuteronomy 7:3 explicitly forbade intermarriage with Canaanites; Samson's dismissal of his parents' valid legal objection shows his characteristic disregard for covenant boundaries even as God sovereignly used the transgression
adults.option_b: The Mosaic law had no provision about intermarriage with Philistines specifically
adults.option_c: His parents were wrong to object because Philistines were not Canaanites under the law
adults.option_d: His parents objected only for social, not religious, reasons
adults.explanation: Judges 14:3 — "Is there never a woman among the daughters of thy brethren...that thou goest to take a wife of the uncircumcised Philistines?" Deuteronomy 7:3 prohibits such marriages specifically to prevent spiritual corruption.

adults.question: What is the literary function of the honey-from-the-carcass episode as a symbol for Samson's life and ministry?
adults.option_a: It encapsulates his whole story — strength and sweetness emerging from death and uncleanness, just as his greatest victory (pulling down the temple) comes through his own death
adults.option_b: It is only a test of cunning with no symbolic significance
adults.option_c: It represents Israel's ability to extract blessing from Canaanite culture
adults.option_d: It symbolises the honey of the Promised Land obtained through warfare
adults.explanation: Judges 14:14 — "Out of the eater came forth meat, and out of the strong came forth sweetness." The riddle becomes an unintended prophecy of Samson himself: a life of violence that paradoxically produces deliverance, culminating in death that saves Israel.

adults.question: How many companions did the Philistines bring to the feast, and what does this detail suggest about the political atmosphere?
adults.option_a: Thirty companions — a number suggesting a heavily armed guard rather than friendly guests, indicating the Philistines mistrusted Samson from the start
adults.option_b: Ten companions who were simply wedding guests
adults.option_c: Seven companions representing the days of the feast
adults.option_d: A hundred companions as a sign of honour to Samson
adults.explanation: Judges 14:11 — "And it came to pass, when they saw him, that they brought thirty companions to be with him." Many commentators note the unusually large number may indicate the Philistines sent these men as guards to watch this feared Israelite.

adults.question: How does Samson's wedding in Judges 14 foreshadow the relationship between Israel and the surrounding nations throughout the book of Judges?
adults.option_a: Just as Samson's marriage to a Philistine brings short-term pleasure but ends in betrayal and death, Israel's repeated entanglement with pagan nations brought momentary alliance but culminated in oppression and spiritual destruction
adults.option_b: Samson's marriage was an exception and has no broader national application
adults.option_c: The marriage ended happily and represents God's desire for Israel to integrate with Canaan
adults.option_d: It is only a personal story with no typological or national dimension
adults.explanation: The cycle of attraction, betrayal, and destruction in Judges 14 mirrors the macro-narrative of Judges 2:1-3, where Israel's entanglement with Canaanite nations leads to serving their gods and being oppressed by them.

kids.question: What animals did Samson use to burn the Philistines' fields?
kids.option_a: Three hundred foxes with torches tied to their tails
kids.explanation: Judges 15:4-5 — "And Samson went and caught three hundred foxes, and took firebrands...and set the brands on fire, and let them go into the standing corn of the Philistines."

kids.question: What unusual weapon did Samson use to kill one thousand Philistines?
kids.explanation: Judges 15:15 — "And he found a new jawbone of an ass, and put forth his hand, and took it, and slew a thousand men therewith."

kids.question: After the great battle, Samson was thirsty. Where did his water miraculously come from?
kids.option_a: From a hollow place in the jawbone
kids.option_b: From a spring that burst out of the ground nearby
kids.option_c: From a well that appeared in the desert
kids.option_d: From rain that fell from heaven
kids.explanation: Judges 15:19 — "But God clave an hollow place that was in the jaw, and there came water thereout; and when he had drunk, his spirit came again."

teens.question: Why did the Philistines burn Samson's wife and her father?
teens.option_a: Because Samson had burned their fields and they blamed her for the trouble Samson brought
teens.option_b: Because she had warned Samson of their plan
teens.option_c: Because she refused to spy on Samson for them
teens.option_d: Because she had run away to Israel
teens.explanation: Judges 15:6 — "And the Philistines came up, and burnt her and her father with fire." They acted exactly as they had threatened in Judges 14:15, fulfilling Samson's prediction that they only used her against him.

teens.question: What did three thousand men of Judah do to Samson, and why?
teens.option_a: They bound him with two new cords and delivered him to the Philistines, fearing Philistine retaliation
teens.option_b: They sheltered him and refused to hand him over
teens.option_c: They brought him to the tabernacle at Shiloh
teens.option_d: They made him their leader to fight the Philistines
teens.explanation: Judges 15:12-13 — "We are come down to bind thee, that we may deliver thee into the hand of the Philistines...And they bound him with two new cords." Judah prioritised peace with Philistia over defending a fellow Israelite.

teens.question: What happened to Samson's bonds when the Philistines came to take him?
teens.option_a: The cords became like burnt flax and he broke free
teens.option_b: He slipped out of the ropes like a snake shedding skin
teens.option_c: An angel cut his bonds with a sword
teens.option_d: The ropes rotted instantly on his wrists
teens.explanation: Judges 15:14 — "the cords that were upon his arms became as flax that was burnt with fire, and his bands loosed from off his hands." The Spirit of the LORD empowered this supernatural breakout.

teens.question: What did Samson name the place where he struck down the thousand Philistines?
teens.option_a: Ramath-lehi, meaning "the hill of the jawbone"
teens.option_b: Gibeah, meaning "the hill of battle"
teens.option_c: Ebenezer, meaning "stone of help"
teens.option_d: Bochim, meaning "the place of weeping"
teens.explanation: Judges 15:17 — "And it came to pass, when he had made an end of speaking, that he cast away the jawbone out of his hand, and called that place Ramath-lehi." The name commemorates the weapon and the victory.

teens.question: How many years did Samson judge Israel according to Judges 15:20?
teens.option_a: Twenty years in the days of the Philistines
teens.option_b: Forty years over all Israel
teens.option_c: Twelve years over Judah only
teens.option_d: Seven years from Zorah
teens.explanation: Judges 15:20 — "And he judged Israel in the days of the Philistines twenty years." This summary statement closes the first major phase of Samson's story.

adults.question: What does the Philistines' burning of Samson's wife and her father fulfill, and what does this reveal about the cyclical logic of vengeance in Judges 15?
adults.option_a: It fulfills the Philistines' own threat from Judges 14:15 and perpetuates a cycle where each act of revenge provokes a greater counter-act, escalating without covenant justice
adults.option_b: It was an act of worship to their gods
adults.option_c: It fulfills a specific prophecy from Deborah's song
adults.option_d: It was Samson's fault for starting the cycle with the foxes
adults.explanation: Judges 15:6-7 — The burning fulfills Judges 14:15; Samson's response in 15:7 — "Though ye have done this, yet will I be avenged of you" — perpetuates the cycle. The narrative shows vengeance as an engine of escalating violence without resolution.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of three thousand men of Judah choosing to hand Samson to the Philistines rather than rally behind him?
adults.option_a: It reveals that Israel had accepted servitude as normal, lacking the spiritual will to seek freedom — a symptom of the covenant abandonment the entire book diagnoses
adults.option_b: It shows that Judah was morally superior to Dan in not provoking Philistia
adults.option_c: It demonstrates that the men of Judah were following Mosaic law about surrendering criminals
adults.option_d: Judah was simply afraid and had no deeper theological meaning
adults.explanation: Judges 15:11 — "Knowest thou not that the Philistines are rulers over us? What is this that thou hast done unto us?" Judah's acceptance of Philistine rule as normal marks a total collapse of the Exodus identity of freedom under God.

adults.question: What does the miracle of water from the jawbone in Judges 15:18-19 reveal about Samson's spiritual condition, and how does it compare to Israel's wilderness experience?
adults.option_a: Like Israel at Meribah, Samson's thirst after victory was met by miraculous divine provision, but he named the spring with possessive language ("God hath given this deliverance into the hand of thy servant") suggesting self-focus even in prayer
adults.option_b: Samson was spiritually mature and the miracle was simply a physical provision
adults.option_c: The miracle shows God no longer needed Samson after the battle
adults.option_d: The comparison with the wilderness is the author's own invention with no textual basis
adults.explanation: Judges 15:18 — "Then he was sore athirst, and called on the LORD, and said, Thou hast given this great deliverance into the hand of thy servant." Compare Exodus 17:3-7 where God provided water after battle. Samson cried to God only in extremity, unlike Moses who interceded on behalf of the people.

adults.question: What is the literary irony of the jawbone of an ass — an unclean animal — being the instrument of divine deliverance?
adults.option_a: God consistently uses the weak, the despised, and the ritually impure as instruments of His power, confounding human expectations of what is fitting
adults.option_b: The jawbone was a common weapon in antiquity and there is nothing unusual about its use
adults.option_c: The use of an unclean item was a deliberate sin that God later punished Samson for
adults.option_d: God blessed the weapon because Samson consecrated it before the battle
adults.explanation: Judges 15:15 — "And he found a new jawbone of an ass...and slew a thousand men therewith." An ass was unclean under Levitical law, yet God empowered its bones as a weapon. This anticipates Paul's "God hath chosen the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty" (1 Cor. 1:27).

adults.question: How does Samson's speech in Judges 15:16 function as a poetic victory song, and what is its literary form?
adults.option_a: It is a brief victory boast in parallelism: "With the jawbone of an ass, heaps upon heaps" — with a wordplay on "chamor" (ass) and "chamar" (heap) — resembling ancient Near Eastern victory poetry
adults.option_b: It is a simple prosaic statement with no poetic features
adults.option_c: It is a prayer of thanksgiving directed entirely to God
adults.option_d: It is a formal legal declaration claiming the land for Israel
adults.explanation: Judges 15:16 — "With the jawbone of an ass, heaps upon heaps, with the jaw of an ass have I slain a thousand men." The repetition and the probable Hebrew wordplay make this a formulaic victory boast comparable to Lamech's sword song (Gen. 4:23-24).

adults.question: What does Samson's naming the spring "En-hakkore" ("the spring of one who called") reveal about his understanding of his own power?
adults.option_a: He attributed the water miracle to God's answer to his cry, showing he knew his strength was divine gift not personal possession — though he did not consistently live this out
adults.option_b: He named it after himself in pride
adults.option_c: He was giving credit to the jawbone that provided water
adults.option_d: The name indicates he thought his own call was the source of power
adults.explanation: Judges 15:19 — "Wherefore he called the name thereof En-hakkore, which is in Lehi unto this day." The name "the spring of him that called" credits God's response to prayer, preserving a moment of genuine dependence amid Samson's generally self-reliant pattern.

adults.question: How does the placement of the summary formula in Judges 15:20 — "he judged Israel twenty years" — in the middle of the Samson narrative rather than at the end affect interpretation?
adults.option_a: It creates a narrative pause before chapter 16's catastrophe, suggesting a period of relative stability before the final tragic arc — implying Samson's story is not simply a linear decline
adults.option_b: It is an editorial error that should appear at the end of chapter 16
adults.option_c: It means chapter 16 is not part of the original Samson narrative
adults.option_d: It summarises all of Samson's activity before chapter 16 begins a new story about a different person
adults.explanation: Judges 15:20 and 16:31 both include the same summary formula. The repetition creates a literary frame for the Delilah episode, marking it as a separate but climactic phase of Samson's judgeship.

adults.question: What does the word "Etam" (a rock cleft where Samson dwelt, v. 8) contribute to the geography of his story?
adults.option_a: It places Samson in Judah's territory, showing the judge of Dan was hiding in a neighbouring tribe's land — suggesting Dan had been so absorbed into Philistine control that even its judge had no secure base
adults.option_b: Etam was Samson's hometown in the tribe of Dan
adults.option_c: Etam was a Philistine city that Samson had captured
adults.option_d: Etam was the location of the tabernacle during this period
adults.explanation: Judges 15:8 — "he went down and dwelt in the top of the rock Etam." Etam is in Judahite territory. Samson, judge of Dan, was effectively a fugitive in Judah, underlining how badly the Philistine occupation had displaced northern tribal life.

kids.question: What was the name of the woman whom Delilah worked for and who paid her to discover Samson's secret?
kids.explanation: Judges 16:5 — "And the lords of the Philistines came up unto her, and said unto her, Entice him, and see wherein his great strength lieth."

kids.question: What was the true secret of Samson's strength?
kids.option_a: His hair had never been cut because he was a Nazirite
kids.option_b: He wore a special golden belt given by God
kids.option_c: He ate from a magical tree in Israel
kids.option_d: He prayed three times every day without fail
kids.explanation: Judges 16:17 — "There hath not come a razor upon mine head; for I have been a Nazarite unto God from my mother's womb: and if I be shaven, then my strength will go from me."

kids.question: What did Samson do at the end of his life that killed him and all the Philistines around him?
kids.option_a: He pushed the two middle pillars of the temple, causing it to fall on everyone inside
kids.option_b: He called fire from heaven on the Philistine army
kids.option_c: He picked up the temple gates and threw them
kids.option_d: He released three hundred foxes with torches inside the temple
kids.explanation: Judges 16:30 — "And Samson said, Let me die with the Philistines. And he bowed himself with all his might; and the house fell upon the lords, and upon all the people that were therein."

teens.question: How much silver did each lord of the Philistines offer Delilah to betray Samson?
teens.option_a: Eleven hundred pieces of silver
teens.option_b: Five hundred pieces of silver
teens.option_c: A thousand pieces of silver
teens.option_d: Two hundred pieces of silver
teens.explanation: Judges 16:5 — "I will give thee every one of us eleven hundred pieces of silver." With five lords, the total amounted to 5,500 pieces of silver.

teens.question: How many false answers did Samson give Delilah before finally telling the truth about his hair?
teens.option_a: Three false answers before the truth
teens.option_b: One false answer
teens.option_c: Five false answers
teens.option_d: He never gave a false answer
teens.explanation: Judges 16:7,11,13 — Samson first said seven green withes, then new ropes, then weaving his hair into a web. Each time he was tested and escaped, yet he told Delilah more truth each time, showing a tragic pattern of progressive disclosure.

teens.question: What two things did the Philistines do to Samson after shaving his head and capturing him?
teens.option_a: They put out his eyes and brought him down to Gaza
teens.option_b: They chained him and paraded him through Philistine cities
teens.option_c: They cut off his hands and displayed him in Ashdod
teens.option_d: They bound him and sacrificed him to Dagon
teens.explanation: Judges 16:21 — "But the Philistines took him, and put out his eyes, and brought him down to Gaza, and bound him with fetters of brass; and he did grind in the prison house."

teens.question: What was the name of the false god to whom the Philistines gave credit for delivering Samson into their hands?
teens.option_a: Dagon
teens.option_b: Baal
teens.option_c: Chemosh
teens.option_d: Molech
teens.explanation: Judges 16:23 — "Then the lords of the Philistines gathered them together for to offer a great sacrifice unto Dagon their god, and to rejoice: for they said, Our god hath delivered Samson our enemy into our hand."

teens.question: What was Samson's final prayer before pulling down the pillars?
teens.option_a: "O Lord GOD, remember me, I pray thee, and strengthen me, I pray thee, only this once, O God"
teens.option_b: "Let me die with the Philistines and avenge my two eyes"
teens.option_c: "LORD God of Israel, grant me one last miracle for Thy glory"
teens.option_d: "O God, forgive my sin and restore me to Thy presence"
teens.explanation: Judges 16:28 — "And Samson called unto the LORD, and said, O Lord GOD, remember me, I pray thee, and strengthen me, I pray thee, only this once, O God, that I may be at once avenged of the Philistines for my two eyes."

adults.question: What does the three-part structure of Delilah's deceptions — each time Samson woke and broke free — suggest about his psychology?
adults.option_a: His repeated return to Delilah after each betrayal shows a compulsive pattern of self-destruction, where he knew she was dangerous yet could not break free — a picture of sin's enslaving nature
adults.option_b: He was testing Delilah to see if she could be trusted
adults.option_c: He was bound by Nazirite law not to abandon a woman he had pledged to
adults.option_d: He did not realise Delilah was working for the Philistines until the fourth attempt
adults.explanation: Judges 16:15-16 — "And she said unto him, How canst thou say, I love thee, when thine heart is not with me? thou hast mocked me these three times...And it came to pass, when she pressed him daily with her words, and urged him, so that his soul was vexed unto death." He knew yet stayed.

adults.question: What is the theological weight of the phrase "And he wist not that the LORD was departed from him" in verse 20?
adults.option_a: It is one of Scripture's most haunting statements — he had so normalised the Lord's presence as background support that its withdrawal went unnoticed, revealing the spiritual blindness that preceded the physical blindness
adults.option_b: It means God had never truly been with Samson
adults.option_c: It means Samson was unconscious when the LORD left
adults.option_d: It is a narrative aside with no deeper theological meaning
adults.explanation: Judges 16:20 — "And she said, The Philistines be upon thee, Samson. And he awoke out of his sleep, and said, I will go out as at other times before, and shake myself. And he wist not that the LORD was departed from him." This verse is the spiritual climax of the Samson narrative.

adults.question: What is the significance of Samson grinding at the mill in the prison house of Gaza?
adults.option_a: Grinding grain was women's work and the most degrading labour in antiquity; his humiliation reverses every marker of his mighty identity — he who tore lions now grinds like a slave woman
adults.option_b: It was a sign that Samson was being rehabilitated by the Philistines
adults.option_c: Grinding was reserved for prisoners of war as honourable labour
adults.option_d: He chose this task voluntarily as a sign of repentance
adults.explanation: Judges 16:21 — "and he did grind in the prison house." The imagery of the mighty judge reduced to slave labour resonates with Isaiah 47:2 where grinding is an image of complete humiliation.

adults.question: How does the narrative detail that Samson's hair began to grow again in verse 22 function as a theological signal?
adults.option_a: It signals the possibility of renewed consecration and divine grace — the vow was being restored even before Samson's final prayer, suggesting grace precedes the sinner's petition
adults.option_b: It is only a biological fact with no spiritual meaning
adults.option_c: It shows that Samson's strength was purely physical and automatic
adults.option_d: It means the Nazirite vow could not permanently be broken
adults.explanation: Judges 16:22 — "Howbeit the hair of his head began to grow again after he was shaven." The placement of this verse before the feast of Dagon and Samson's final prayer suggests the narrator intends the reader to see grace already moving toward restoration.

adults.question: What is the typological significance many see in Samson's death — dying with outstretched arms between two pillars to destroy God's enemies?
adults.option_a: It is seen as a type of Christ dying on the cross with arms outstretched, destroying the power of the enemy through death, killing more in dying than in all his life
adults.option_b: There is no typological connection and the comparison is theologically unsound
adults.option_c: It typifies the defeat of Israel by Philistia
adults.option_d: It typifies Moses lifting his arms at the battle of Amalek
adults.explanation: Judges 16:30 — "So the dead which he slew at his death were more than they which he slew in his life." Many patristic and Reformation commentators saw Samson's death between the pillars as a type of Christ's atoning death.

adults.question: What does Judges 16 reveal about the spiritual condition of Israel that its judge is the prisoner of its oppressors, grinding in their prison, before his final act of deliverance?
adults.option_a: It concretises the spiritual reality of Judges 2: Israel had become enslaved through covenant infidelity, and even its judge bore the marks of that slavery, making his final act a compressed picture of Israel's own need for redemption
adults.option_b: It is simply a personal failure of Samson with no national symbolism
adults.option_c: It shows that the Philistines were spiritually superior to Israel at this time
adults.option_d: It fulfills the curse of Deuteronomy 28 on Samson alone, not on all Israel
adults.explanation: Judges 16:21 — Samson blinded, bound, grinding. Deuteronomy 28:29,33 warns that covenant apostasy would result in groping in darkness and serving enemies. Samson's condition embodied the national spiritual state.

adults.question: How does Delilah function narratively as a parallel to Samson's Timnite wife in chapter 14?
